item.title: Dharmette: On not understanding dharma talks
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to a listener who sometimes understands Dharma talks immediately and sometimes feels left out of them. He reflects on how teaching always involves judgment calls, why recognition can feel like remembering, and why not understanding can itself be part of wisdom. The talk gradually shifts toward patience, humility, and a practice of learning without needing to be right.

item.title: Equanimity 6
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver describes equanimity as a real practice that first takes effort and then, at times, opens into a much deeper letting go. He distinguishes ordinary balance from the Mahasi-style stage of “equanimity with formations,” where experience feels less sticky, the three characteristics are vivid, and the sense of self loosens into space and groundlessness. Toward the end, he pauses on the risks of turning insight into a state to chase, and returns to the practical question of how this changes a life of commitments and compassion.

item.title: Equanimity 5
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why spiritual practice rarely fits a neat personal story, then turns that same humility toward equanimity: how it is noticed, strengthened, and limited in ordinary life. He connects equanimity with not-self, the draining of egoic charge, and the practical need to respect when a mind is too depleted to do much more than stop and regroup. The talk closes by widening equanimity into a care for others, where holding pain becomes part of love, solidarity, and remorse.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Equanimity 4
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a short, practice-oriented talk on equanimity as a companion to compassion rather than a dry or distant state. He begins with an alternating metta and equanimity exercise, then develops the theme through the experience of loving when we cannot control outcomes. The talk closes by bringing the teaching into everyday and civic life: where care is best spent, and how to remain open without over-identifying with suffering.

item.title: Equanimity 3
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a careful, practical talk on equanimity as a way of meeting pleasant and unpleasant experience without bracing, suppression, or over-identification. He moves from broad reflections on how suffering shapes our narratives into a more detailed account of sensory balance, then brings in several contemporary and traditional teachers to sharpen the definition. The second half turns more inward and practical, with questions about difficult emotions, shame, jealousy, and the slow ripening of practice over time.

item.title: Equanimity 2
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores equanimity as a way of meeting experience without turning every sensation into a problem to solve. He traces how the mind reads events through self-relevance, how pleasant and unpleasant feelings trigger strategy and control, and why relaxing the impulse to manage each moment can reveal a simpler flow of arising and passing. The talk closes with a repeated settling into letting go, making it especially useful for listeners interested in how equanimity works in practice, not as indifference but as a softer relationship to feeling.
Chapters: 03:34 Self-Relevance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=214 05:37 Pleasant and Unpleasant - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=337 07:02 Alarm and Strategy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=422 09:24 What Equanimity Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=564 13:18 Desperation Around Pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=798 16:01 Relaxing the Bracing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=961 17:26 Letting Go Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=1046
Source: AudioDharma

He traces how the mind reads events through self-relevance, how pleasant and unpleasant feelings trigger strategy and control, and why relaxing the impulse to manage each moment can reveal a simpler flow of arising and passing. The talk closes with a repeated settling into letting go, making it especially useful for listeners interested in how equanimity works in practice, not as indifference but as a softer relationship to feeling.
Chapters: 03:34 Self-Relevance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=214 05:37 Pleasant and Unpleasant - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=337 07:02 Alarm and Strategy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=422 09:24 What Equanimity Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=564 13:18 Desperation Around Pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=798 16:01 Relaxing the Bracing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=961 17:26 Letting Go Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22308/?t=1046

item.title: Equanimity 1
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores equanimity as something more active and intimate than calm or passivity: a way of meeting unpleasantness, helplessness, and feeling without trying to control experience. He moves from examples in retreat life, parenting, politics, and prison work to a practical claim that equanimity softens reactivity, reduces the pressure of preference, and supports wiser action. The talk includes short audience reflections and a long guided sitting that turns toward bodily sensation, models of self and world, and the felt sense of urgency.
Chapters: 01:20 What retreat teaches - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=80 05:18 First meanings of equanimity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=318 11:40 Softening instead of freezing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=700 19:35 Helplessness and wise action - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=1175 29:25 Sitting with experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=1765 40:32 Friction falls away - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=2432 54:45 Love it, not escape it - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=3285 58:42 Feeling at the center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22307/?t=3522
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: The mirage of self evaporates as the fires cool
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens with a quote from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to explore nibbana as cooling, spaciousness, and the easing of compulsive selfing. He moves between Buddhist teaching, reflections on lay practice, and a few outside references to show why liberation is less about improving the self than about letting the fires of craving and fear settle. The talk ends by turning toward a quieter, less territorial sense of freedom that leaves more room for care.
Chapters: 00:16 Ajahn Sucitto's frame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=16 02:23 Practice turns inward - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=143 04:52 Mara and conditioning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=292 06:19 Openness instead of denial - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=379 08:38 Space and cooling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=518 10:45 The self cannot own freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=645 12:50 What remains - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22252/?t=770
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Remembering Ann Buck
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet remembrance of Ann Buck, beginning with a morning scene, a Dylan lyric, and the feeling of mortality that opened the talk. From there he moves through memories of Ann’s practice life, her fierceness and warmth, and the way her presence made Dharma feel ordinary and real. The talk ends by widening from personal grief to a meditation on how the living might honor the dead through memory, steadiness, and continued love.
Chapters: 00:09 Morning, Dylan, mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=9 01:14 Ann’s passing and example - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=74 03:10 Life in the ordinary - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=190 05:01 Cling, then let go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=301 06:40 Her direct kindness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=400 08:13 Dharma through conversation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=493 10:30 Mind, aging, and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=630 12:50 What the dead might want - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22216/?t=770

item.title: Dharmette: To Care and Not to Care Simultaneously
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores T.S. Eliot’s phrase “teach us to care and not to care” as a lived Dharma question, not a slogan. He moves from the alternating rhythms of compassion and rest to a more radical possibility: caring and not caring at the same time, as an expression of emptiness and release. The talk then turns to sutta imagery of perception thinning out and consciousness no longer landing anywhere, ending with a quiet suggestion that this freedom may still feel benevolent and warm.
Chapters: 00:09 Eliot and the question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=9 02:02 Love and rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=122 04:57 Beyond sequence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=297 06:59 Refining perception - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=419 08:47 Less and less - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=527 09:58 Where mind does not land - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=598 12:01 Quiet closing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22138/?t=721
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Unsustainable Trajectories & Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with the Buddha’s recognition that a life organized around pleasure was unsustainable, then widens that lens to social, technological, and ecological conditions that can also feel unstable. He sits with fear, anger, and the pull toward nihilism, but keeps returning to a steadier response: practice, hope stretched across longer time scales, and a commitment to love, non-harming, and compassion. The talk closes with a local story about a rescued goose on Mount Shasta, which becomes a small but affecting image of care and spiritual friendship.
Chapters: 00:09 Unsustainable beginnings - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=9 02:00 A larger trajectory - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=120 03:21 Anger and confusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=201 05:06 Powerlessness and nihilism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=306 07:38 Freedom and hope - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=458 09:14 Planting long seeds - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=554 10:52 Back to fundamentals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=652 11:38 The goose on Shasta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22101/?t=698
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Truth & Lies in the US in 2025
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on truthfulness as a Dharma practice, starting from his childhood anger at hypocrisy and moving into the political atmosphere of the United States in 2025. He distinguishes lying from “bullshit,” connects honesty to care for others, and argues that both self-deception and social deception weaken our capacity to see clearly. The talk closes with practical invitations: treasure truth, give up dependence on the world honoring it, and find healing in sangha, where reality does not need to be denied.
Chapters: 00:00 Hypocrisy and early anger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=0 02:37 Honesty as moral care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=157 04:24 Lies and bullshit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=264 06:33 Truth in practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=393 07:25 Political outrage cycle - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=445 10:20 Treasure the truth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=620 12:24 Seeing self-deception - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=744 13:17 Sangha and letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22046/?t=797
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: more of our personality in freedom than in our suffering
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a reversal of Tolstoy’s famous line: suffering can feel intensely personal, but freedom may reveal more of our actual uniqueness. Drawing on Buddhist teaching, Carl Rogers, Winnicott, and Bruce Tift, he explores how empathy, relinquishment, and reduced compulsivity allow a more spontaneous, distinctive self to appear. The talk stays grounded in the felt contrast between the performative quality of suffering and the quiet freshness of liberation.
Chapters: 00:17 Tolstoy Reversed - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=17 01:48 Pain Feels Unique - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=108 04:55 Meeting Others Carefully - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=295 07:28 What Freedom Looks Like - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=448 09:28 True Self and Gesture - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=568 11:53 Already Enlightened - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=713 13:27 Closing Questions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22017/?t=807
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Attention and Anicca, Awareness and Anatta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the difference between attention and awareness, using humor, retreat stories, and quoted teachers to show how practice changes the way experience is known. He moves from attention as a form of love and prayer into the ways attention reveals impermanence, selfing, and the limits of control. The talk gradually turns toward awareness as something dependently arisen and not owned by a self, ending with a quiet emphasis on surrender, non-identification, and the simplicity of knowing.
Chapters: 00:01 From Fixing to Listening - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=1 02:22 Attention as Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=142 05:43 Unmixed Attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=343 09:51 Right Now, Like This - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=591 16:00 What Awareness Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=960 21:33 How Attention Shapes Experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=1293 28:44 Attention and Impermanence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=1724 34:35 Awareness Without Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=2075 40:21 Surrendering the Center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21943/?t=2421

item.title: Dharmette: It's Complicated and It's Simple
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens by dwelling on the sheer complexity of mind, life, and the systems we live within, drawing on neuroscience and cosmology to suggest that much of existence remains beyond our full grasp. He then turns toward a quieter frame: a retreatant’s simple insight that life may come down to love and heartbreak, and the Buddha’s deeper distinction between suffering and peace. The talk settles into a humane invitation to love what is here, let the heart break, and meet that with care and wisdom rather than confusion.
Chapters: 00:09 Life Is Complicated - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=9 01:02 Brains and Networks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=62 02:39 Stories We Tell - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=159 04:12 The Reach of the Cosmos - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=252 06:15 Love and Heartbreak - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=375 06:59 Right Distinctions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=419 09:26 No Cure for Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=566 11:57 Yielding to Time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=717
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21935

He then turns toward a quieter frame: a retreatant’s simple insight that life may come down to love and heartbreak, and the Buddha’s deeper distinction between suffering and peace. The talk settles into a humane invitation to love what is here, let the heart break, and meet that with care and wisdom rather than confusion.
Chapters: 00:09 Life Is Complicated - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=9 01:02 Brains and Networks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=62 02:39 Stories We Tell - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=159 04:12 The Reach of the Cosmos - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=252 06:15 Love and Heartbreak - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=375 06:59 Right Distinctions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=419 09:26 No Cure for Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=566 11:57 Yielding to Time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21935/?t=717
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (5 of 5) Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver closes his Divine Abodes series with a talk on equanimity as a form of rest: not numbness, but a way of caring that can actually continue. He moves through the tension between compassion and helplessness, drawing on Ajahn Buddhadasa, Ajahn Sucitto, and T. S. Eliot to show how equanimity can purify care without hardening the heart. The talk is quiet and practical, with a steady emphasis on balance, sustainability, and the middle path between over-involvement and apathy.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening gratitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=12 01:57 Ease and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=117 03:38 Still, not rigid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=218 05:05 The cost of caring - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=305 07:27 Heart at rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=447 09:40 Caring and not caring - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=580 11:31 Purifying compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=691 14:03 Helplessness and goodwill - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21889/?t=843
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (4 of 5) Sympathetic Joy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver focuses on mudita, or sympathetic joy, as a practice that cannot be forced but can be cultivated by loosening the body and softening the grip of self-view. He contrasts genuine delight in others with forced happiness, then works through the inner obstacles of envy, possessiveness, and comparison. The talk closes by framing another person's happiness as something the heart can simply partake in, without threat or loss.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving at Mudita - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=12 00:57 Joy Cannot Be Forced - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=57 03:24 Resentment and the Sutta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=204 05:41 Joy Beyond Scarcity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=341 08:00 Letting Down the Burden - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=480 10:26 What Blocks Joy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=626 12:58 Jealousy and Envy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=778 14:34 Sufficient and Unthreatened - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21874/?t=874
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette; The Divine Abodes (3 of 5): Compassion
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores compassion as love in the face of dukkha, moving from the shock of suffering to a more nuanced understanding of its causes. He brings in the Buddha, Hala Alyan, and Katherine Schultz to show how grief, impermanence, and dependent arising can open into a steadier, wiser care. The talk stays close to the question of how to love without collapsing into enmeshment, despair, or advice-giving.
Chapters: 00:16 Suffering Is Everywhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=16 00:52 Deep and Hard to See - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=52 01:48 The Question of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=108 03:57 What Compassion Is - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=237 06:26 Loss Reveals Impermanence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=386 09:22 Not Enmeshment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=562 11:22 Grief Held by Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=682 14:21 Wise and Steady Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21867/?t=861
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Mindfulness: Acceptance and Change
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores a common Dharma tension: whether practice is about accepting ourselves or changing ourselves. Using short teaching stories and quotes from Niels Bohr, Suzuki Roshi, Marsha Linehan, Kobun Chino Roshi, and William James, he argues that real mindfulness holds both truths together without turning acceptance into a strategy. The talk moves through shame, self-view, and the craving to become someone else, then settles into a quiet case for non-shame as the basis for honest transformation.
Chapters: 00:12 The central question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=12 00:55 A pointed teaching story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=55 02:06 Two profound truths - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=126 03:08 Acceptance without bargaining - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=188 04:59 Shame and self-view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=299 06:21 Sitting with reality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=381 07:45 The craving to become - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=465 09:05 Freedom from shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=545 10:35 Change from acceptance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21865/?t=635
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Dereification of Self-Model
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores emptiness as the progressive dereification of the self-model: the way thoughts, preferences, and ownership stop feeling like reality and become seen as passing phenomena. He begins with a childhood thought experiment about everything falling away, then moves into a more technical explanation of how identification, craving, and selfing are built. The talk closes by linking equanimity, quiet, and a greater comfort with not knowing.
Chapters: 00:18 A Childhood Thought Experiment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21870/?t=18 04:18 What Dereification Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21870/?t=258 07:14 Thoughts Become Objects - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21870/?t=434 09:33 Layers of Selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21870/?t=573 12:17 Ownership and Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21870/?t=737 14:11 Quiet and Not Knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21870/?t=851
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (2 of 5) Metta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores metta as something distinct from liking or wanting: not another form of preference, but a different stance of the heart. He traces how fear and craving contract the mind, then shows how loving-kindness can become an approach to pain, a purification practice, and a way of abiding with less control and more openness. The talk closes by linking metta to moral elevation, the recognition of goodness, and the possibility of resting in a warm, spacious heart.
Chapters: 00:35 Love beyond liking - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=35 01:49 Wanting and fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=109 03:36 Metta as approach - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=216 05:38 What love is not - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=338 06:59 Attachment and family - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=419 10:19 Goodness as source - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=619 11:39 Moral elevation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=699 13:23 Abiding in love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21857/?t=803

item.title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (1 of 5) Orienting to Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet, practical reflection on the Divine Abodes as a way of orienting the heart toward love rather than hate. He begins with the felt difference love makes when life is imperfect, then moves through non-hatred, ethical conduct, and the four Brahma-viharas as trainable resting places for the mind. The talk closes by inviting listeners to notice where aversion appears and to meet it with flexibility, improvisation, and a steadier return to love.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Believing that Special People Exist
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with a line from Rachel Kushner and uses it to explore why people imagine that special people exist. From there, he turns toward idealization, childhood dependence, ambivalence, and the difference between revering goodness and deifying a person. The talk ends by linking this to sympathetic joy and to practicing for the right reasons rather than for applause.
Chapters: 00:17 The question of specialness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21823/?t=17 02:30 Projection and childhood - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21823/?t=150 04:46 Growing up unresolved - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21823/?t=286 06:57 Reverence without ownership - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21823/?t=417 09:49 Sympathetic joy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21823/?t=589 11:06 Practice without applause - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21823/?t=666
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Fear and Love and Dana
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores fear as a useful survival system that can also overlearn danger and narrow the heart. Using the story of Joao, a man who became radically generous after a stroke, he asks how fear constrains love, generosity, and our sense of what matters. The talk closes by returning to Thich Nhat Hanh on meeting fear directly, suggesting that courage and honest awareness can soften worry and open a more radical love.
Chapters: 00:09 Fear Keeps Us Safe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=9 02:44 Fear Of Suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=164 03:38 Joao’s Sudden Generosity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=218 05:54 Brains And Freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=354 07:55 What Love Might Be - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=475 09:37 Social Rules And Self-Consciousness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=577 11:55 Meeting Fear Directly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=715 13:24 Courage And Radical Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21804/?t=804
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Aperture of Attention in Meditation and Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses the image of an aperture to explore how attention can narrow or widen in meditation and in daily life. He traces when a smaller field is stabilizing, when a wider one is called for by compassion or service, and why the skill lies less in ideology than in timing. The talk stays practical and reflective, with a quiet emphasis on noticing what is wise now rather than forcing one mode of practice.
Chapters: 00:19 Aperture of Attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=19 01:33 Settling In - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=93 02:57 Opening With Stability - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=177 04:00 Life Beyond Meditation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=240 05:51 Contraction and Compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=351 08:10 When Narrowing Helps - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=490 10:45 When Wide Attention Heals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=645 11:49 Wise Attention Now - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21764/?t=709

item.title: Dharmette: Sensitivity to Suffering vs. Fear of Suffering
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver speaks candidly about grief, political aftermath, and the strain of trying to care without becoming overwhelmed. He moves from the Buddhist middle path to a quieter distinction between sensitivity to suffering and fear of suffering, then asks what courage might make possible in compassion. The talk stays personal and reflective, with a few literary and philosophical references used to clarify that arc.

item.title: Brief Post-Election Reflections
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a brief, quiet reflection on waking up after the 2024 election, beginning with the ground of practice and spiritual friendship before moving into grief, uncertainty, and the thin line between stability and collapse. He draws on Michelle McDonald, Mother Teresa, Leonard Cohen, the Buddha, and Shinzen Young to explore how heartbreak can deepen care without slipping into naivete or brittle equanimity. The talk gradually turns from personal processing to the question of what a more mature, nonviolent form of engagement might become.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving Together - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=0 01:19 What This Moment Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=79 02:56 Thinness and Uncertainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=176 05:15 Grief and Naivete - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=315 07:09 Heartbreak That Opens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=429 09:16 Sorting Pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=556 11:39 Comprehending Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=699 13:52 Beyond Equanimity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=832 15:56 A Nonviolent Protest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-87036/?t=956
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Dharmette: Post-Election Reflections
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the morning after an election, using the shared shock as a starting point for spiritual friendship, grief, and clearer seeing. He moves through impermanence, the thin line between safety and danger, and the way heartbreak can deepen love without becoming naivete. The talk turns toward what it means to comprehend dukkha, how to care without collapsing, and how practice may ask for a larger form of nonviolent engagement.
Chapters: 00:17 Gathering in practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=17 01:23 Holding the shock - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=83 03:09 Anicca and the thin line - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=189 05:22 Grief that teaches - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=322 08:09 Love after heartbreak - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=489 12:07 Comprehending dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=727 14:45 No complacent resting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=885 16:09 Closing offer - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21671/?t=969

item.title: Dharmette: Giving Love the Last Word
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens with a philosophical analogy about fever and uses it to frame anger as a protective response rather than something to suppress or glorify. From there he returns to a refrain: anger may be the first word, but love should be the last, even in fear, conflict, and uncertainty. The talk moves toward nonself, the limits of control and domination, and a closing ethic of blessing others through everyday contact.
Chapters: 00:19 Fever and Anger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=19 02:25 Love the Last Word - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=145 05:23 The Koan of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=323 08:12 Not Knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=492 09:16 Releasing Ownership - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=556 10:24 Relief and Openness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=624 11:43 Every Contact Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=703 12:39 Closing Blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21624/?t=759

item.title: Love & Rest Daylong - Introduction to theme
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens this daylong by naming the strain of the current political moment and the way news and social media can pull the heart into fear, reactivity, and helplessness. He asks whether care must always come with so much suffering, then explores sangha, moral clarity, and the possibility of discharging stress without abandoning love. The talk moves toward a practical middle way: staying open but not overexposed, and using disciplined practice to love and rest more wisely.
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the strain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=0 03:35 Sangha and moral clarity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=215 08:57 The heart under pressure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=537 10:46 Disentangling distress - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=646 14:38 Love without overexposure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=878 18:56 Suffering effectively - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=1136 20:27 Practice instructions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=1227 40:43 Freedom in restraint - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21698/?t=2443
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Love & Rest Daylong - Between Nihilism and Frenzy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how practice often asks us to distinguish wholesome pain from unnecessary suffering, and how love can become clearer without becoming cold. He moves through the tension between compassion and equanimity, asking how to care deeply without collapsing into control, grief, or exhaustion. The talk keeps returning to a middle path between nihilism and frenzy: spacious enough to hold heartbreak, honest enough to admit helplessness, and steady enough to keep loving.

item.title: Dharmette: Personality Disorder at the Individual and National Level
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses the language of personality disorder to explore rigidity, defensiveness, comparison, and cruelty in self-view, then broadens that lens to American political culture. He speaks candidly about how such dynamics pull clinicians and ordinary people into counter-transference, frenzy, and moral confusion, while pointing toward non-clinging, wholesome action, spiritual friendship, and a longer view as ways to stay sane. The talk stays grounded in clinical observation but keeps returning to Dharma practice as a discipline of softening and clear-hearted response.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening With Candid Presence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=12 00:36 Seeing Thick Suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=36 01:38 Ego Taken To Extremes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=98 03:08 Rigidity And Self-View - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=188 05:19 Comparison And Cruelty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=319 07:10 Clinician In The Current - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=430 09:03 From Patients To Politics - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=543 12:12 How To Stay Sane - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21565/?t=732
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Attention and Anicca, Awareness and Anatta (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the difference between attention and awareness through a retreat talk that moves from the felt practice of listening to a more philosophical account of consciousness and selfhood. He uses Simone Weil, Thomas Nagel, Ajahn Sumedho, William James, and others to show how attention can become prayerful, how it can also tighten into effort and self-making, and how practice can soften into receptive knowing. The talk closes by circling back to anatta: the loosening of the sense of a fixed center, and the possibility that love, more than control, is what remains compatible with emptiness.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into listening - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=0 06:07 Attention as prayer - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=367 09:37 Conscious experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=577 12:23 Below awareness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=743 20:00 How attention works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=1200 25:55 Aiming and sustaining - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=1555 29:02 Impermanence and selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=1742 35:17 Resting in awareness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=2117 39:52 Non-identification and love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86636/?t=2392
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Craving, Compulsion, Non-Freedom (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a retreat talk on craving, compulsivity, and the narrow way we often mistake suffering for something to be fixed. He starts with the hindrances, then widens into a longer inquiry into dukkha, freedom, and why the mind keeps reaching for “next.” The talk moves through classical Dharma language, contemporary psychology, and a few literary and scientific references to show how practice can reveal a freedom that is not another form of grasping.

item.title: Dharmette: Crowdsourced Themes - Death, Grief, Anxiety, Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to listener themes around death, grief, anxiety, love, and equanimity by moving from personal stories into a steady reflection on mortality. He treats death contemplation as a way to clarify what matters, pre-grieve loss, and meet impermanence without surprise, while also acknowledging how fear and grief remain part of practice. The talk widens near the end toward collective suffering, compassion, and the limits of any single life to hold the world’s pain.

item.title: Dharmette: What is the Function of a Dharma Talk?
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what a Dharma talk is actually for: not just information, but a way of teaching that can clarify, unsettle, gladden, and invite practice. He moves from a classical sutta on how teachings should be offered into a more relational view of Dharma as co-meditation, attention, and shared sincerity. By the end, the talk frames teaching as a transmission that happens in a shared field, where speaker and listeners help create the meaning together.
Chapters: 00:00 Starting the Question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=0 01:42 The Classical Counsel - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=102 02:59 More Than Information - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=179 04:55 Faith and Uncertainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=295 07:05 Gladden the Mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=425 09:39 Attention as Care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=579 12:00 Clearing Space - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=720 14:02 Transmission and Sangha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21413/?t=842
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Plasticity, Call off the search, Unification and Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on freedom as something more varied than suffering, then organizes the talk around four “neighborhoods” of liberation: plasticity, calling off the search, unification, and love. He moves from psychological flexibility and dependent arising to the fading of compulsive becoming, the healing of inner division, and a more measureless, communal form of love. The talk closes by widening the moral imagination, drawing on Frederick Douglass and Abraham Heschel to suggest that love can be both tender and prophetic.
Chapters: 01:05 Suffering and freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=65 05:18 Four neighborhoods - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=318 07:11 Plasticity and flexibility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=431 11:36 Loose self-story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=696 16:23 Mind before the world - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=983 20:23 Calling off the search - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=1223 27:37 Unification and breadth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=1657 34:16 Love without measure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=2056 39:42 Prophetic vastness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86542/?t=2382

item.title: Emptiness of Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores emptiness of self through a simple practice of listening: to the body, to feeling, and to the impulses that want to fix or control experience. He then turns to questions about innocence, metta, and spiritual friendship, using the Buddha’s exchange with Ananda to suggest that friendship is a way of meeting what is present, not just a relationship between people. In the closing section, he offers a careful reflection on how equanimity can purify compassion, especially when love gets tangled with urgency, fear, or control.

item.title: Balancing Equanimity and Compassion
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores compassion as love in the face of suffering, then gradually brings it into conversation with equanimity. The talk moves from a brief settling and reflection on attention, into a guided compassion practice, and then toward the question of how to stay open without becoming frenzied or collapsed in the face of helplessness. It closes with a quieter, wider view of love that does not depend on fixing everything, and a final invitation to let that quality shape even the way we look and move.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Intention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=0 01:30 Preparing the Heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=90 05:32 Meeting Suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=332 12:25 Offering Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=745 18:17 Shared Human Ground - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=1097 20:28 Compassion and Equanimity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=1228 27:21 Refining Compulsion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=1641 30:39 Love in Daily Life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86538/?t=1839

item.title: Grace, Gravity, Surrender and Silence
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver builds this talk around Leonard Cohen’s image of living between gravity and grace, then turns it into a practical Dharma inquiry into suffering, helplessness, effort, and surrender. He moves from biography and literary quotation into meditation practice, asking how faith, receptivity, and silence change our relationship to control. The talk gradually settles into a quieter argument: that letting go is not passivity, but a way of meeting impermanence without losing the heart.

item.title: Relaxing the Body and Mind
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a gentle instruction in relaxing the body and then the mind, beginning with small areas of tension and widening into the felt sense of the whole body. He emphasizes that relaxation is not about making experience pleasant, but about loosening the urge to manage, acquire, or defend against whatever is arising. The talk then turns toward the breath as something received in a field of ease, and later to walking practice, where he uses his own difficulties and a story from Jack Kornfield to show how stillness and spaciousness can meet despair.

item.title: Metta for Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a talk and guided practice on metta for oneself, beginning with a broad framing of love as something that makes human life bearable. He distinguishes the four Brahma-viharas as different species of love, then spends much of the talk showing how self-mettta can work as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting whatever pain arises. The practice settles into repeated phrases of blessing and a steady invitation to see oneself through kind, merciful eyes, ending with a sense of love as vast and measureless.
Chapters: 00:34 Love and human life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=34 01:51 Four kinds of love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=111 04:00 How metta works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=240 06:29 What metta reveals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=389 09:25 Finding your own path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=565 12:28 A merciful gaze - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=748 18:10 Blessing the self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1090 24:52 Problems dissolving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1492 29:14 What has not been loved - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1754

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver offers a talk and guided practice on metta for oneself, beginning with a broad framing of love as something that makes human life bearable. He distinguishes the four Brahma-viharas as different species of love, then spends much of the talk showing how self-mettta can work as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting whatever pain arises. The practice settles into repeated phrases of blessing and a steady invitation to see oneself through kind, merciful eyes, ending with a sense of love as vast and measureless.
Chapters: 00:34 Love and human life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=34 01:51 Four kinds of love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=111 04:00 How metta works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=240 06:29 What metta reveals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=389 09:25 Finding your own path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=565 12:28 A merciful gaze - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=748 18:10 Blessing the self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1090 24:52 Problems dissolving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1492 29:14 What has not been loved - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86532/?t=1754

item.title: Dharmette: Between Gravity and Grace
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses Leonard Cohen’s writing as a lens for dukkha, practice, and the uneasy beauty of living between falling and grace. He reflects on why people practice when it is not always comforting, how motivation changes over time, and why the Dharma can feel less like an answer than a relationship with wisdom and love. The talk stays close to the tension between honesty about suffering and a quiet trust that practice can still matter.

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (5 of 5): A Training in Loving Well
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a formative question from Jack Kornfield’s Path with Heart — "Did I love well?" — and uses it to shift the frame from moment-to-moment choice toward long-term training. He argues that ethical conduct, attention, and wisdom gradually reshape the heart so that love becomes less reactive, more steady, and more attentive to others. The talk stays grounded in ordinary interpersonal life, with a quiet emphasis on patience, micro-choices, and the slow accumulation of inner strength.

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (4 of 5): Honoring the Neurosis/Pain of the Other
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how suffering is understood in the Four Noble Truths, then turns that lens toward relationship: how another person’s pain can evoke compassion, fear, control, or resentment. He explores the need to hold empathy and boundaries together, so care does not collapse into compulsive fixing or emotional contagion. The talk closes with a Leonard Cohen line that frames the wish for some kind of truce between competing loves.

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on Sexuality
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on sexuality as a neglected Dharma topic, moving carefully through its beauty, harm, and instability. He asks what it might mean to bring attention, ethics, and spiritual practice into intimacy without pretending the territory is simple. The talk weaves together psychoanalytic ideas, poems and stories, and practical questions about shame, selfhood, and freedom.

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (3 of 5): Assertion and Surrender
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores relationships as a lived practice of both assertion and surrender, using the images of water and ice to describe when to soften, when to hold boundaries, and when to say no. He draws on a retreat exercise with Ajahn Sucitto and his own awkwardness in paired practice to show how intimacy can reveal both openness and the need for individuation. The talk ends by returning to the practical ethics of yes and no: how trust, consent, and self-respect depend on being able to move between connection and boundary.

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (2 of 5): Not-Knowing and the Wildness of Loving Another Person
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how interpersonal Dharma holds both knowing and not knowing: empathy, self-understanding, and the surprise of discovering that ourselves and others are never fully captured by our concepts. He moves from mindfulness as self-directed empathy to forgiveness, then into the ways love seeks safety, routines harden into flatness, and intimacy asks us to stay open to the other’s wildness. A brief reference to Simone Weil, Stephen Mitchell, and a passage from Philip Roth’s The Human Stain deepens the talk’s central tension between human predictability and the living mystery of relationship.
Chapters: 00:18 Who Am I? - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=18 02:23 Empathy and Not-Knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=143 04:53 Forgiveness Opens Space - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=293 06:16 Seeing Loved Ones Anew - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=376 08:20 Safety and Deadness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=500 10:01 The Limits of Knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=601 12:10 Wildness in Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21225/?t=730
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Life (1 of 5): Introduction
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens a week on interpersonal life by framing practice as a movement from egocentric living toward a life animated by Dharma. He emphasizes that relationship is not a side topic but a place where belonging, suffering, healing, and wisdom become visible, and where practice is tested and made real. The talk moves through reflections on mutual influence, the nervous system, and personal stories that show how other people reveal both our conditioning and our capacities for care.

item.title: Dharmette: Reflection vs. Rumination
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the difference between reflection and rumination, beginning with the way thoughts can be useful but are often asked to do too much. He then traces how rumination loops around pain, regret, and uncertainty, trying to solve emotional problems with words alone. The talk ends with a practical turn toward mindfulness, embodied feeling, and sometimes simply putting the mind down, moving, or seeking support from spiritual friendship.

item.title: Sunday Dharma Talk
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the Eightfold Path as both a map and a lived unfolding, moving between humility about how little of a life we can fully explain and confidence that practice can still be taught. He weaves in Krishnamurti, Joseph Goldstein, Rumi, Mary Oliver, and Adrienne Rich to explore the tension between structure and freedom, insight and gradual cultivation, and private practice and social responsibility. The talk gradually turns from wise view to intention, ethics, and the body’s slow training toward less harm and more love.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the Eightfold Path as both a map and a lived unfolding, moving between humility about how little of a life we can fully explain and confidence that practice can still be taught. He weaves in Krishnamurti, Joseph Goldstein, Rumi, Mary Oliver, and Adrienne Rich to explore the tension between structure and freedom, insight and gradual cultivation, and private practice and social responsibility. The talk gradually turns from wise view to intention, ethics, and the body’s slow training toward less harm and more love.
Chapters: 00:30 Ancient path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=30 01:51 Life’s hidden currents - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=111 06:46 Path and freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=406 09:19 Trusting the teaching - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=559 11:25 Experience is not path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=685 16:25 Wisdom and illusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=985 23:15 Refining view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=1395 29:14 Ambivalence and aspiration - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=1754 34:18 Training the body - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21182/?t=2058
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Friday Dharma Talk
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the First and Second Noble Truths through the felt difference between craving and desire, arguing that suffering is often normalized before it is clearly seen. He moves from personal reflection and humor into neuroscience, psychoanalytic writing, and Buddhist sources to show how wanting, clinging, and a divided life keep us looped in frustration. The talk turns practical near the end, emphasizing patience, equanimity, dharma pleasures, and the possibility of loosening subtler forms of selfing.
Chapters: 00:01 Normalizing Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=1 02:16 Seeing Suffering Clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=136 05:05 Compulsivity And Cause - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=305 09:43 Craving And Desire - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=583 16:32 Wanting Versus Liking - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=992 20:34 Unlived Lives - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=1234 27:04 Patience And Insight - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=1624 32:26 Equanimity And Subtle Clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=1946 44:43 Relaxing The Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21171/?t=2683

item.title: Formal Opening Part 2 (welcome and theme)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens the retreat by inviting listeners to meet whatever arises with acceptance, and by treating even restlessness and uncertainty as part of practice. He then names a broader shift from life as a problem to life as a lesson, and frames the retreat around the Four Noble Truths. The talk turns into a detailed, quiet explanation of refuge and the five precepts, with a short personal story about an open church door as an image of shelter and openness.

item.title: Dharmette: Meaning and Purpose
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores meaning and purpose through a practical lens, beginning with a Seneca line about having a destination before asking which parts of life are inherently meaningful. He then draws on self-determination theory to name three basic needs — autonomy, competence, and relatedness — and shows how Dharma practice can strengthen each one. The talk closes by turning from self-focus toward love, friendship, and service as sources of purpose.

item.title: Moral Elevation, Moral Disgust and the Vastness Required to Open to Dukkha
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can sensitize us to goodness without losing contact with suffering. He moves from moral elevation and the force of ordinary courage into moral disgust, asking how care can stay clear of hatred, cynicism, and political reactivity. The talk gradually widens into a reflection on vastness, mortality, and impermanence, ending with Ajahn Chah’s tea cup as a way to hold life with gratitude and less fear.

item.title: Introduction to Theme - Abiding in Care
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames this talk around a simple but demanding question: what kind of happiness are we actually living for? He moves from the tension between feeling good and feeling bad into a spacious account of love, showing how the Brahma-viharas, renunciation, and even painful experience can all become part of abiding in care. The talk ends in a quieter register, with vipassana described as radical non-violence toward experience and mindfulness as remembering what matters.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the day - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=1 04:39 Secret philosophy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=279 10:34 Anger and its limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=634 14:23 The Brahma-viharas - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=863 24:33 Understanding as love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=1473 30:30 Many paths to care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=1830 37:27 Non-violence toward experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=2247 46:28 Closing reminders - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-21111/?t=2788

item.title: Dharmette: Measurement and the Immeasurable
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how the comparing mind turns people into objects, and how that habit feeds pride, shame, envy, and a sense of lack. He then widens the frame to measurement itself, drawing on sutta language to show how greed, aversion, and delusion depend on making themes and distinctions. The talk closes with the Brahma-viharas and the “immeasurable” quality of loving-kindness, suggesting a relief that comes when measurement falls silent.

item.title: Dharmette: Craving>Suffering, Suffering>Craving
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses a listener question about Goenka to explore craving not just as a cause of suffering, but as something that suffering itself can call forth. He moves from Buddhist causality to Stephen Batchelor’s reversal, then brings in George Koob’s addiction research to show how craving, disappointment, and negative affect can reinforce one another. The talk ends with practical ways of working with craving: noticing its pain directly, renouncing for a time, and discovering that less may actually be enough.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=30 01:31 Dharma as causality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=91 03:06 Suffering as cause - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=186 03:50 The reverse loop - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=230 05:26 Craving from within - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=326 07:14 What we really want - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=434 08:36 Addiction parallel - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=516 11:47 Working with craving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=707 14:09 Enough already - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20988/?t=849

item.title: Dharmette: The Wholesome Disorientation of Feedback
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on feedback as a practical mirror for seeing self-clinging, defensiveness, and the places where practice is still tender. He tells a few humorous, slightly exposing stories, then broadens into a simple map of head, heart, and belly as different capacities that can develop unevenly. The talk ends by treating surprise and disorientation not as a problem to avoid, but as part of the path of growth and self-understanding.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening with feedback - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=12 01:10 Defensiveness and anatta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=70 02:37 The Kinko's story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=157 05:19 Head, heart, belly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=319 07:31 Teachers and students - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=451 08:46 Growth we miss - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=526 10:28 Startled by change - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=628 12:21 Love beyond discovery - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20922/?t=741

item.title: Dharmette: Between Everything Means Everything and Nothing Means Anything
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how disappointment with institutions can turn into either cynicism or a deeper, more spacious disenchantment. He traces a path through heartbreak, nihilism, and the Buddhist language of samsara and dukkha toward a view that is wide enough to hold suffering without making everything absolute. The talk closes by linking that vastness to equanimity: a big, peaceful love that no longer tries to control what cannot be controlled.
Chapters: 00:12 Early disappointment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=12 02:01 Dukkha at the group level - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=121 03:59 Mesmerized by samsara - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=239 05:57 Beyond nihilism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=357 07:26 The universe's view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=446 09:01 Emptiness and poignancy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=541 10:40 Love without control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=640 12:22 Equanimity as love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=742

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how disappointment with institutions can turn into either cynicism or a deeper, more spacious disenchantment. He traces a path through heartbreak, nihilism, and the Buddhist language of samsara and dukkha toward a view that is wide enough to hold suffering without making everything absolute. The talk closes by linking that vastness to equanimity: a big, peaceful love that no longer tries to control what cannot be controlled.
Chapters: 00:12 Early disappointment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=12 02:01 Dukkha at the group level - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=121 03:59 Mesmerized by samsara - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=239 05:57 Beyond nihilism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=357 07:26 The universe's view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=446 09:01 Emptiness and poignancy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=541 10:40 Love without control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=640 12:22 Equanimity as love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20865/?t=742
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20865

item.title: Not-self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores not-self as a practical loosening of the burdens built into selfing, using comparisons from psychotherapy, narrative identity, shame, and meditation practice. He traces how the sense of self is repeatedly maintained through effort, comparison, and fear of being seen, then points toward a quieter mode of awareness that is less owned and less defended. The talk closes by suggesting that as clinging drops away, personality can appear more fully and more naturally, with more ease and less strain.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=0 01:17 Healing has limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=77 04:03 What experience is like - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=243 08:35 Narrative gravity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=515 13:28 Why self is tiring - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=808 17:26 Shame and comparison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=1046 21:53 Humor and fluidity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=1313 25:20 Truthfulness and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=1520 30:20 Practice without a doer - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86530/?t=1820

item.title: Healing the Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores healing as a flexible relationship among self-love, self-acceptance, and self-forgetting rather than a fixed sequence. He moves from shame and fear into the role of faith, sangha, and mindfulness as conditions that make change possible, then turns toward how the past is carried, digested, and gradually rewritten as a Dharma story. The talk stays grounded in ordinary psychological life while keeping liberation in view: a stable, fluid self that can live with both suffering and ease.
Chapters: 00:00 Three modes of healing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=0 04:21 Why letting go feels risky - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=261 10:46 Insight and backlash - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=646 17:14 Healing as acceptance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=1034 24:37 What we really want - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=1477 32:22 Story, presence, and the past - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=1942 39:48 Too much to digest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=2388 43:25 A stable, flexible self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86528/?t=2605
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Throughlines of Self and Not-Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores practice as a movement between pleasantness and necessary difficulty, arguing that the path reduces suffering while also making room for fulfillment, delight, and wholesome discomfort. He then turns to the tension between self and not-self, suggesting that health depends on flexibility: being able to inhabit a personal story without becoming trapped in it, and to taste non-egoic awareness without losing groundedness or care. The talk closes by emphasizing two balancing qualities—renunciation and patience—as supports for a Dharma that deepens gradually and changes character over time.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=0 01:17 Pleasure and suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=77 03:08 The fruit of practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=188 05:42 Necessary suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=342 10:20 Stress reduction and self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=620 13:33 Flexibility of awareness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=813 20:14 Power and safety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=1214 24:26 Renunciation and enough - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=1466 30:43 Patience through the flood - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-86526/?t=1843

item.title: Love and Space
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet reflection on practice as a way of stepping out of measurement, autobiography, and the habit of making the self into an object. He repeatedly returns to themes of spaciousness, embodiment, and loving attention, suggesting that liberation is less about fixing experience than about relating to it without the usual sorting and selfing. The talk moves from instruction into a more meditative refrain, with repeated phrases that invite listeners to lay down striving and meet experience with an open heart.

Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Deep Embodiment
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quietly insistent talk on inhabiting the body before turning toward the story of the self. He begins with gravity, breath, and the felt sense of aliveness, then repeatedly returns to the ways the mind tries to manage unpleasantness, uncertainty, and the urge to fix. The talk gradually opens into a simpler view: life and death are not fully owned or governed, and this moment can be received as Dhamma rather than solved.

item.title: Dharmette: Non-clinging
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver answers a question about how to overcome clinging by first questioning the idea that letting go can be reduced to a simple method. He frames non-clinging as something shaped by conditions, reinforcement, and the whole fabric of practice, drawing on Joseph Goldstein, Shantideva, and the Buddha to show why willpower alone is not enough. The talk ends with a practical, humane view: letting go may require resolve, patience, equanimity, and sometimes a redirection of love rather than a clean release.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=0 01:32 What Clinging Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=92 02:53 Why No Simple Method - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=173 04:18 Conditions and Consent - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=258 05:56 Reinforcement and Habit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=356 08:29 The Paramis of Release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=509 12:10 Reinvesting Energy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=730 14:01 Living With Loss - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20759/?t=841
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Mindfulness, Mindlessness, Rumination and the Species of Unconsciousness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a gentle, practical talk on mindfulness as something richer than a technique: a way of loosening identification with thought, softening distress, and making room for awareness, silence, and the body. He distinguishes ordinary mind-wandering and rest from the more corrosive forms of rumination that keep suffering going, and he returns repeatedly to equanimity as the condition that lets attention settle. In the question period, he speaks carefully about intrusive thoughts, remorse, and self-forgiveness, emphasizing that thoughts are conditioned and need not define character.

item.title: Dharmette: Crying
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects quietly and personally on crying in Dharma practice, beginning with his own tears while teaching and asking what they mean. He considers whether tears are disclosure, burden, or manipulation, then turns to research, memory, grief, joy, and the way practice can open both sorrow and tenderness. The talk ends by linking tears to embodied awareness and to the harder work of change, not just feeling.
Chapters: 00:12 A talk on crying - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=12 01:32 What tears might mean - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=92 03:13 Words and research - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=193 05:02 Need and helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=302 06:15 Practice opens grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=375 08:03 Teaching from the body - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=483 09:56 Grief, solidarity, goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=596 12:00 Moral elevation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=720 13:51 Tears are not enough - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20694/?t=831

item.title: Dharmette: Love and Time
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a blessing he was asked to offer at a family rite of passage, and uses it to think aloud about how love and time belong together. He argues that finitude makes love matter, grief is part of a spiritual life, and time becomes meaningful when it is lived in relationship. The talk closes by widening the lens to many forms of love, including the Brahma-viharas and the particular love that emerges in family and close relationships.
Chapters: 00:09 A blessing request - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=9 00:48 Love and time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=48 02:14 Finitude and care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=134 03:25 Grief in love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=205 04:43 Love across years - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=283 06:01 Time needs love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=361 07:26 Love holds together - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=446 08:47 Many faces of love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20628/?t=527
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Functions of dharma pleasure
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the role of wholesome pleasure in Dharma practice, beginning with a story about a longtime practitioner who argues that people need teaching on pleasure, not only impermanence and suffering. He then names several kinds of Dharma pleasure—path, sangha, ethical alignment, non-shame, love, concentration, and reverence—and explains how they support steadiness, broaden attention, and help practice continue. The talk closes by suggesting that pleasure can make room for purification and for digesting pain, rather than feeding craving.
Chapters: 00:09 Why teach pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=9 02:07 Kinds of Dharma pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=127 03:22 Non-shame and love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=202 05:46 Concentration and reverence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=346 07:06 What pleasure does - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=426 09:09 Pleasure as support - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=549 12:06 Broadening awareness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=726 13:53 Making room for pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20586/?t=833
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on words from Ajahn Sucitto
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short teaching from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to unfold a quiet map of the path: generosity, ethics, renunciation, discernment, persistence, and patience. He moves from the felt meaning of sharing to the practical question of how to direct energy, make choices, and time action well. The talk stays grounded in ordinary examples and in the sense that practice is less about intensity than about steady, responsive care.

item.title: Dharmette: Cruising Altitude and Upheaval
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the changing rhythms of practice, especially the difference between quieter stretches of stability and the more disruptive periods when a familiar view no longer fits. He begins with grief and the limits of trying to analyze feeling too quickly, then expands into a longer analogy with Thomas Kuhn’s idea of normal science and scientific revolution. The talk settles into a careful invitation to notice when life or practice has outgrown an old story, and to treat that rupture as part of the ongoing path.
Chapters: 00:10 Practice in phases - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=10 02:34 Grief and spaciousness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=154 04:22 Coming home, leaving home - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=262 06:54 Kuhn’s normal science - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=414 09:01 When the story breaks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=541 11:27 Self-view under pressure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=687 13:26 Revolution and rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20469/?t=806
Source: AudioDharma
