item.title: Overconfidence As A Defilement
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores overconfidence as a spiritual and psychological problem, linking it to attachment to views, self-deception, and the ways certainty hardens into harm. He moves from Buddhist teachings on anicca and delusion into moral uncertainty, the limits of knowing, and the need for humility in personal and public life. The talk closes by turning toward embodied awareness and a careful relationship to feeling, where care can remain open without being captured by clinging.

item.title: Brian Lesage, Cara Lai, Matthew Brensilver: Closing Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
This closing session moves from a guided landing into the body, to a small-group speaking practice, and then into reflections on how retreat practice carries into daily life. The teaching emphasizes gradual change, sensitive integration, ethical clarity, and the need to meet transition without forcing it. The session ends with a communal closing, forgiveness, and a blessing for the goodness cultivated together.

item.title: Language is the hammer and every problem, a nail (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how much of meditation practice is shaped by the mind’s ongoing attempt to manage experience, interpret feeling, and solve the problem of self. He moves from vedana and restlessness into the limits of language, the habit of narrating life, and the relief that can come from not immediately turning every sensation into a project. The talk gradually opens toward acceptance, awareness, and the idea that some problems need solving while others need to dissolve.

item.title: Dharmette: Coming Home and Leaving Home
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a movement between coming home and leaving home: returning to what the heart already knows, then learning to love new things. He explains that transformation usually happens gradually, through small repeated gestures rather than a single decision, and uses Plato, Alfred Adler, and a cited philosopher to make that case. The talk ends by returning to the image of a “new home,” where practice slowly reshapes what feels familiar and worth loving.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Fear and Language
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the uneasy link between fear and language, suggesting that much of thinking is not just a cause of suffering but also a response to it. He moves from the value of words, to the mind’s urge to reduce uncertainty, to the way clinging, comparison, and selfing are organized through language. The talk closes by pointing toward a quieter trust: letting go, receiving phenomena as invitations to release, and placing faith in silence and awareness.
Chapters: 00:09 Words and nourishment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=9 01:23 Silence and language - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=83 03:06 Fear beneath thought - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=186 04:50 Anicca and uncertainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=290 07:14 Clinging and self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=434 09:37 No problems - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=577 11:31 Trusting release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=691 13:36 Closing blessings - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=816

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on a RJ Davidson Quote
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short passage from R.J. Davidson and colleagues to unpack what mindfulness training is said to cultivate: steadier attention, meta-awareness, de-reification, non-reactivity, and a loosening of self-story. He then moves from the language of the research into a more contemplative register, using images of attention, gravitational pull, and event horizons to describe how thought can absorb experience and how practice helps us wake up from that pull. The talk closes by turning to equanimity and the softer, less sticky relationship to self that can emerge when identification relaxes.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short passage from R.J. Davidson and colleagues to unpack what mindfulness training is said to cultivate: steadier attention, meta-awareness, de-reification, non-reactivity, and a loosening of self-story. He then moves from the language of the research into a more contemplative register, using images of attention, gravitational pull, and event horizons to describe how thought can absorb experience and how practice helps us wake up from that pull. The talk closes by turning to equanimity and the softer, less sticky relationship to self that can emerge when identification relaxes.

item.title: Dharmette: The Clinging Before Clinging
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how clinging can begin long before obvious craving or aversion appears. Drawing on the Dhammapada, dependent origination, and a Zen saying about the disappearing subject, he traces how subtle volitional habits shape perception, selfing, and the feeling that the world has hardened into fixed things. The talk closes by pointing toward relinquishment: a simpler, less comparative way of meeting experience that is both disenchanted and quietly amazed.
Chapters: 00:12 Returning to old loves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=12 00:48 Mind and idealism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=48 02:12 Experience is shaped - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=132 03:25 Clinging before clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=205 05:00 Models that never idle - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=300 06:39 Formative energies - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=399 08:03 Making the world solid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=483 10:44 Relinquishment and simplicity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=644 13:03 Wonder without selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=783
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Becoming Unintimidated by Suffering
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what it means to meet suffering without fear, beginning with the experience of listening to pain that is both sacred and ordinary. He traces how pain gets bound up with shame, why being witnessed can be healing, and how genuine compassion depends on becoming less intimidated by our own suffering. The talk closes by returning to the Buddha’s instruction to comprehend dukkha: know it in awareness, and let that understanding open into innocence, confidence, and care for others.
Chapters: 00:09 Meeting pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=9 02:12 Pain and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=132 05:16 Witnessing without fixing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=316 06:46 Norman Fischer on compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=406 08:47 Not missing dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=527 10:30 Awareness and space - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=630 12:17 Innocence and confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=737
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: New Questions
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on teaching, the shaping force of sangha, and how Dharma learning can depend on being both settled and unsettled. He moves from a personal, self-effacing opening into a meditation on pedagogy, cognitive impasses, and the way familiar understandings crack open into new questions. The talk closes by asking what new language the Dharma gives us for self, love, death, and consolation.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Letting Go (5 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why Dharma practice can feel risky: it asks us to stop managing, stop over-reading, and enter a more vulnerable relationship with experience. Drawing on William James, Nisargadatta, Akena, and Ajahn Sucitto, he frames letting go as a courageous descent out of the personal world rather than a simple technique for feeling better. The talk moves toward a quiet claim that faith is what allows that descent, and that even a brief moment of trusting awareness can change the heart.
Chapters: 00:09 James and tragic depth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=9 02:13 What the path offers - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=133 03:31 Trying something new - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=211 04:54 Risk of letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=294 06:47 Power in helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=407 07:55 Effective suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=475 09:08 The rope of release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=548 11:31 Fewer signposts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=691 13:09 Faith and gratitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=789

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Ourselves (4 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores what it means to have faith in oneself without building up a flattering story of self. He argues that real confidence comes through anatta, the lessening of self-deception, and a quieter trust in direct knowing, bodily cues, and intuitive discernment. The talk moves from skepticism about self-esteem language to a practical account of how to become more accountable to what we already know.
Chapters: 00:12 Self-esteem revisited - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=12 01:41 Faith beyond story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=101 03:19 Not-self and confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=199 05:23 Trusting body and mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=323 06:45 Intuitive knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=405 08:15 Less self-deception - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=495 09:40 Subtle cues and data - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=580 11:14 Accountable to knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=674
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Love (3 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a kind of faith that is not certainty, but trust in love as the deeper response to suffering, aversion, and fear. He opens with a tender poem by Louise Cook, then turns to the psychology of hatred, the necessity of open-heartedness, and the way practice can reveal that deep happiness depends on love. In the latter half, he uses dependent arising and a reference to Martha Nussbaum to show how following emotion all the way through can lead not to hate, but to kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Poem - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=12 01:07 Faith and Doubt - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=67 02:02 Love Against Aversion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=122 04:17 Open-Hearted Happiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=257 06:20 Anger’s Dead End - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=380 07:59 Dependent Arising - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=479 10:27 Emotion and Need - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=627 11:46 Following to Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=706
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Emerging From Doubt
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores doubt as a kind of disorientation: the mind keeps reaching for certainty, but the more it grasps for answers, the more it can get tangled in thought, comparison, and self-conscious practice. He then turns toward faith—not as belief in a theory, but as a growing trust in direct experience, patience, and what is actually trustworthy. The talk closes with a practical encouragement to stay open amid intensity and let the path unfold without demanding impossible certainty.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening and Theme - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=12 00:57 Doubt as Disorientation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=57 03:01 Narratives and Rumination - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=181 05:01 What Practice Cannot Do - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=301 07:38 Precision and Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=458 10:04 Trusting Experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=604 10:59 Faith as a Hinge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=659 12:15 Closing Encouragement - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=735
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Faith in Our Dharma Path (1 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on faith as something quieter and sturdier than belief or optimism. He traces his own suspicion of the word, then shows how practice can become a surrender that does not deny pain or uncertainty. The talk moves toward trust in awareness, truth, love, and a sense of belonging in the Dharma rather than in borrowed views.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Not-self and self expression
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores not-self as a practical loosening of performance, shame, and fixed identity. Starting from a literary image of masks, he moves through social presentation, self-hatred, and the pressure of self-view, then turns toward flexibility, relational trust, and a more embodied sense of being real. The talk argues that insight into anatta does not erase personality; it frees goodness, weirdness, and sincerity to show up without clinging.

item.title: Mindfulness, Mind-wandering, and Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames mindfulness as a practical training in learning what the mind does, rather than a quest for silence or special states. He moves from the pull of mind-wandering and self-referential thought to the role of sustained attention, de-reification, and non-reactivity, and then broadens into equanimity as the quality that lets suffering crest and soften. The talk closes by showing how this wider, steadier relationship to experience reduces identification with story and helps pain become something metabolized rather than spilled onto others.
Chapters: 00:27 Mindfulness as learning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=27 01:53 What we actually want - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=113 04:50 The allure of unconsciousness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=290 08:18 Mind wandering as baseline - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=498 14:11 Continuity without strain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=851 22:27 Anchoring and noticing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=1347 30:16 Thought, space, and reactivity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=1816 38:20 Equanimity and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=2300 44:29 Less story, more life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=2669

item.title: Dharmette: Compassion & Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores compassion and equanimity as a paired practice rather than separate ideals. He moves from the Buddha’s nuance and resistance to fundamentalism, into the willingness to feel suffering vividly, then into a quiet kind of love that can rest without collapsing into apathy or blame. The talk closes by describing an alternating rhythm of love and rest, where each supports the other.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Passivity & Action
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores a common misunderstanding of equanimity: that being receptive or passive means not acting. He argues instead that receptivity can help us see more clearly, choose more wisely, and make our actions more effective, especially when we understand craving, impermanence, and our own limits. The talk also turns toward love, self-knowledge, and how good will changes the way we move through the world and relate to others.
Chapters: 00:12 Equanimity Is Not Apathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=12 01:25 Passivity in Service - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=85 02:25 Retreat as Experiment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=145 04:30 Action Becomes More Effective - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=270 06:22 Seeing Impermanence Clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=382 08:06 Forecasting and Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=486 10:11 Knowing Our Limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=611 12:17 Respecting Others Carefully - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=737 14:24 A Virtuous Cycle - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=864

item.title: Dharmette: Disenchantment & Engagement
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how Buddhist practice can hold two movements at once: disenchantment with samsara and a real care for the world. He begins with the vulnerability of human life, turns to the Bahhiya Sutta and the idea of samvega, and then asks how renunciation and engagement fit together in a modern lay path. The talk closes by arguing that letting go of grasping can make room for compassion, not indifference.

item.title: Dharmette: Effort & Effortlessness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the tension between effort and effortlessness in Dharma practice, arguing that early training requires energy but that practice cannot be sustained by willpower alone. He describes how effort can tangle with self-view, how habits and the nervous system gradually become allies, and how awareness and wholesome action can become more natural over time. A cited passage from William James helps frame the talk as a movement from strain toward trained ease.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Confidence & Humility
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores confidence and humility as complementary qualities in Dharma practice, rather than opposites. He moves from the idea that wisdom is context-sensitive to the ways practice becomes more trustworthy, more ordinary, and less divided from life. The talk ends by linking humility with epistemic caution, nonviolence, and a willingness to keep looking.

item.title: Welcome - Abiding in Care
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens with a quiet welcome and then reflects on what practice changes: less project-management of life, less fixation on past and future, and a more organic resting in the present. He uses an overheard line from Gil Fronsdal about ambition and vision to question his own relationship to purpose, then turns toward sangha, care, and the way a Dharma life may be lived into rather than engineered. The talk later becomes more explicitly embodied, drawing on Ilan Moffat’s phrase “becoming available to the Dharma,” and ends by bringing wisdom and love together as two wings of awakening.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening Welcome - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1 00:47 Life Less Managed - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=47 03:03 Ambition and Vision - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=183 04:44 A Record of Care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=284 07:23 Living Into Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=443 10:52 Entering Sangha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=652 17:23 The Form of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1043 22:07 Becoming Available - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1327 31:44 Wisdom and Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1904

item.title: Dharmette 2 - Love, Helplessness, Equanimity, and Affect
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses early Buddhist passages, modern philosophy, and recent research to look at love as something real, costly, and often inseparable from helplessness. He moves from the Buddha's fear and compassion toward a wider question: how to care without turning care into ego, despair, or a demand that life keep improving. The talk gradually settles into equanimity as a way of staying open at the edge of what we can control, and ends with a vivid reading of Philip Roth on finitude and aliveness.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Setup - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=0 01:11 Fear and the Thorn - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=71 04:27 Love and Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=267 08:20 Widening Empathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=500 11:36 Compassion Costs - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=696 14:25 Progress and Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=865 18:58 Grief and Finitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1138 25:29 Honest Ethical Seeing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1529 31:45 Spending Love Wisely - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1905
Source: AudioDharma

He moves from the Buddha's fear and compassion toward a wider question: how to care without turning care into ego, despair, or a demand that life keep improving. The talk gradually settles into equanimity as a way of staying open at the edge of what we can control, and ends with a vivid reading of Philip Roth on finitude and aliveness.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Setup - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=0 01:11 Fear and the Thorn - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=71 04:27 Love and Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=267 08:20 Widening Empathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=500 11:36 Compassion Costs - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=696 14:25 Progress and Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=865 18:58 Grief and Finitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1138 25:29 Honest Ethical Seeing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1529 31:45 Spending Love Wisely - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1905

item.title: Dharmette 1 - Aspects of Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores love as more than a feeling: a bodily tone, a state of mind, and a way perception itself gets colored. He moves from the difference between intense and quiet forms of love into how love changes attention, softens self-absorption, and alters what seems prominent or threatening. The talk closes by inviting a simple practice of “ringing the bell” of metta and noticing the reverberations, with the Brahma-viharas framed as a complete set of medicine for experience.
Chapters: 00:01 Love Beyond Feeling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=1 01:05 Feeling and Mind State - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=65 02:36 Where Love Lives - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=156 04:59 Intensity and Quietness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=299 07:04 Feeling Shapes Consciousness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=424 09:32 Love Infuses the View - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=572 14:00 Space, Bias, and Fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=840 17:57 Practice and Return - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=1077

item.title: Sila and Our Debt to Others
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on sila as the place where Dharma practice meets other people: speech, action, livelihood, and the practical shape of our debt to one another. He moves from precepts and non-harm into questions of helplessness, grief, and how compassion can become sustainable when balanced by equanimity. The talk widens into civilizational responsibility, then closes with impermanence and the urgency of spending love wisely.

item.title: Intensity, Feeling, Thinking as Coping, Insight
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how retreat can make experience feel more intense rather than less, and how that intensity often gets managed through thinking, story, and explanation. He traces a quiet path through feeling, attention, equanimity, faith, and insight, suggesting that practice is often less about getting somewhere than about meeting what is already here without fighting it. Along the way he weaves in references to Ajahn Sucitto, the Buddha, Sharon Salzberg, Shinzen Young, and others to show how embodied feeling and nonconceptual awareness work together.
Chapters: 00:03 Living dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=3 01:48 Retreat as intensification - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=108 03:46 Energy and samadhi - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=226 06:57 Naming the intensity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=417 12:43 Feeling at the center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=763 18:08 Attention and soothing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=1088 24:17 Do nothing practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=1457 31:04 Equanimity and faith - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=1864 38:37 Stories and insight - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=2317

item.title: Duality, Merging, Not-Self and Differentiation in the Interpersonal World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how self-acceptance, not-self, and intimacy meet in the relational world, using a retreat exercise of walking toward another person as his central example. He moves from the felt awkwardness of contact into questions of projection, shame, empathy, and the ethical force of another person’s face. The talk then widens into a careful balance between merging and differentiation: how to love without losing clarity, how to care without being consumed, and how equanimity can make compassion more spacious and effective.
Chapters: 00:35 Sitting and acceptance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=35 01:48 Not-self and lucidity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=108 03:22 Lay life and relationship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=202 07:55 Approaching another - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=475 11:38 The face and ethics - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=698 17:29 Self in the gaze - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1049 21:46 Seen as merely seen - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1306 25:23 Ego, safety, and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1523 31:10 Play and feeling real - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1870

item.title: Knowing and Not-Knowing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on teaching from the inside of uncertainty, using a retreat-planning story and a cliffside teaching image to make not-knowing the entry point for practice. From there he unfolds a quiet argument that Dharma asks for both confidence and humility, for knowing and for relinquishing the need to know. The talk moves through ignorance, self-study, love, and the role of surrender in insight and samadhi.

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Randy Fernando: Navigating A.I. from a Buddhist Perspective
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando frame AI as a Buddhist problem of attention, suffering, and conditions rather than novelty alone. The talk moves from a brief opening sit, into a sober overview of generative AI and its risks, and then into a Dharma lens on dependent co-arising, sense-making, and the First Noble Truth. It closes with questions about anatta, the future of teaching and practice, and a return to the Brahma Viharas as a steadier ethical orientation.

item.title: The anchor is everywhere
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores meditation not only as a way to stabilize attention, but as a way to reveal the movement of mind itself. He turns from the familiar use of breath as an anchor toward the idea that awareness has no fixed center, so that everything can become part of the field of knowing. The talk closes by returning to non-identification, forgiveness, and the practical instruction to rest where awareness cannot be stopped.
Chapters: 00:00 What an anchor does - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=0 02:03 Balance and transparency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=123 04:07 What if it's everywhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=247 11:19 From circle to field - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=679 12:29 Unpartitioned knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=749 20:57 Non-identification - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=1257 23:23 Forgiveness and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=1403 29:31 Rest where awareness stays - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=1771
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Surrender & Faith (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores surrender as a practical part of Dharma rather than a passive giving up. He begins with the feeling of always adjusting life’s knobs, then contrasts willfulness with willingness, and moves into how retreat can reveal helplessness, faith, and the limits of control. Along the way he draws on Augustine, Ajahn Sucitto, William James, Emerson, and Ajahn Chah to show how letting go can make room for insight, mindfulness, and wisdom.
Chapters: 00:00 The urge to control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=0 02:37 A Christian contrast - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=157 04:06 Surrender in Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=246 09:45 Conditioning and effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=585 17:35 Helplessness and faith - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=1055 24:50 Trusting the descent - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=1490 31:58 Mindfulness as release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=1918 38:28 Opening to change - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=2308
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Cycling through All Four Divine Abodes
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a guided exploration of the four Brahma-viharas as a cycle of related kinds of love: loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity. The talk moves from recognizing who most needs metta, to meeting suffering without burnout, to rejoicing in ordinary goodness, and finally to a quiet, spacious love that can remain present when life cannot be controlled. He emphasizes that these practices support one another, and that even when one quality is missing, another may be the right doorway.

The talk moves from recognizing who most needs metta, to meeting suffering without burnout, to rejoicing in ordinary goodness, and finally to a quiet, spacious love that can remain present when life cannot be controlled. He emphasizes that these practices support one another, and that even when one quality is missing, another may be the right doorway.

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality - Fear, Helplessness & Self (4/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores mortality as a way to clarify value, loosen willfulness, and look directly at helplessness. He moves from the felt loss of control around death into a meditation on how selfing arises from fear, then turns to not-self as a help, though not a perfect consolation, for death and loss. The talk closes with Derek Parfit’s reflection on the “glass tunnel” of selfhood and the possibility of living more openly and with less separation.
Chapters: 00:09 Mortality Clarifies Value - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=9 00:48 Helplessness and Selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=48 01:51 Death as Humbling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=111 03:14 The Mirage of Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=194 05:08 Training With Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=308 07:29 Loss and Vastness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=449 08:50 Self as Effect - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=530 10:49 Not-Self and Death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=649 12:04 Parfit and Open Air - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=724

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality and the Clarification of What Matters (3/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how modern life floods us with information while leaving us without a story, and he uses that contrast to ask what keeps us from losing the plot. From there he turns toward mortality as a practical contemplation: not to frighten us, but to clarify what matters, loosen old resentments, and make life feel more complete. The talk moves through retreat experience, a David Whyte story, and an interview with Roland Griffiths to show how death can help expose false refuge and deepen wakefulness.
Chapters: 00:17 Noise and Information - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=17 01:54 Losing the Plot - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=114 04:03 A Life in a Moment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=243 05:09 Remembering Death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=309 07:48 Wakefulness in Illness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=468 09:25 Honey and Poison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=565 11:38 Practicing Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=698
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality - Futurelessness & Samadhi (2/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores mortality as a practical contemplation rather than a grim abstraction, asking how death can reveal the mind’s constant orientation toward the future. He moves from William James and the Buddha to a contemporary scientific framing, then back to lived practice: settling the heart, pruning counterfactual thinking, and resting in what is here now. The talk ends by linking deep surrender with the ordinary task of shaping a Dharma life.

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on Mortality (1/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on mortality as a form of practice: something to learn from without either exaggerating it or flattening it. He moves from Ajahn Chah’s reminder that everything teaches us, to the idea that finitude gives life meaning, and then into personal stories about a recent family death, a year-to-live practice, and a difficult conversation with a Holocaust survivor neighbor. The talk settles on humility before the mystery of death, and on how mortality can inform love, ethics, concentration, and the sense of self.

item.title: The Three Characteristics and Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how retreat can make the heart more porous, so that insight does not stay abstract but becomes a felt opening to love. He moves from the three characteristics—dukkha, impermanence, and not-self—toward the way these teachings soften defensiveness, grief, and self-clinging. The talk gradually lands in a quiet claim: when ownership loosens, what remains is a very ordinary, stable kind of love.

item.title: Dharma Talk: Delusion on the Path
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores delusion not as a single mistake, but as a whole pattern of mind: self-deception, identification with thought, confabulated stories, and the subtle ways greed and aversion get justified. He moves from retreat as a form of travel, through Buddhist and psychological models of mind-wandering, toward a quieter practice of receptivity, humility, and learning from experience. The talk ends by loosening the grip of self-view and asking what wakefulness looks like when we are willing to be wrong.

item.title: Dharmette: Acclimatizing to Anicca
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores anicca as something more than the fact that things end: it is the ongoing process of change, unreliability, and arising-and-passing away. He moves from the mind’s wish to stabilize life and avoid surprise, to the ways meditation reveals our habit of tracking self, story, and trajectory. The talk then turns toward a gentler response: stability, gratitude, equanimity, and a kind of resilience that can meet impermanence without hardening.

item.title: Reflections on Ajahn Sucitto Talk
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto story about visiting a brother with advanced illness, using it to explore what remains meaningful when words, memory, and control fail. The talk moves from embodied care and mettā to mortality, family, and the limits of empathy, then toward the quieter relief of equanimity. Along the way he brings in Nick Cave, Cormac McCarthy, Carl Rogers, and Ajahn Chah to show how practice can stay close to the body and open to suffering without being overwhelmed by it.
Chapters: 00:59 Ajahn Sucitto's visit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=59 04:01 What remains after loss - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=241 08:09 Mortality as messenger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=489 11:49 Body over abstraction - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=709 18:12 Kindness without words - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=1092 23:11 Love made vast - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=1391 28:08 Limits of compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=1688 33:12 Equanimity and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17856/?t=1992
Source: AudioDharma

The talk moves from embodied care and mettā to mortality, family, and the limits of empathy, then toward the quieter relief of equanimity. Along the way he brings in Nick Cave, Cormac McCarthy, Carl Rogers, and Ajahn Chah to show how practice can stay close to the body and open to suffering without being overwhelmed by it.

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma Practice & Beginning Again
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on practice as something non-linear, iterative, and repeatedly renewed. He moves from mindfulness as memory to the ordinary ways we forget, make mistakes, get discouraged, and then begin again in practice, in relationships, and in life. The talk closes by softening perfectionism and encouraging a simple commitment to start, continue, and trust the path without needing to finish it all at once.
Chapters: 00:51 An Iterative Path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=51 02:32 Beginning Again - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=152 05:21 Remembering the Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=321 06:57 Messes and Forgiveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=417 08:15 Relationships and Loose Ends - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=495 09:20 No Perfect Finish - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=560 10:32 Changing Intentions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=632 12:17 Each Moment Anew - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17756/?t=737
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma & Grief
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on grief as a core part of Dharma rather than a feeling to bypass. He moves from the Buddha’s encounter with mortality to the ways grief shows up in letting go, ethical growth, and widening compassion. The talk closes by suggesting that grief can be fluid and transformative, even “pregnant with bodhicitta.”

item.title: Dharmette: Humor and Playfulness in the Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how humor and playfulness can coexist with the seriousness of Dharma practice, and why that matters for deepening rather than trivializing the path. He begins with a retreat-center update and a story about being called “Captain Buzzkill,” then builds a case that ego tends toward defensiveness and seriousness while play opens a more easeful, less self-conscious way of being. The talk closes with a vivid personal account where grief, laughter, and peace arise together, suggesting that these qualities are not in conflict.

item.title: Dharmette: Surrender in the Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores surrender as a Dharma practice that is distinct from blind submission or passive resignation. He moves from a Leonard Cohen reflection on the search dissolving, through Augustine’s language of restlessness and dependence, into a Buddhist account of relaxing willfulness, meeting not-knowing, and allowing stillness to change the mind. The talk gradually widens surrender from an ethical attitude into a practical way of relating to mindfulness, samsara, insight, and silence.

item.title: Dharmette: Awe in the Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet talk on awe as a real support for Dharma practice: a way of meeting the path with wonder rather than grasping. He moves from a psychological definition of awe to retreat memories, the rarity of human birth, the emptiness of convention, and the way awe can shrink the self and open the world. The talk stays grounded in ordinary practice, asking how wonder might become a cleaner engine for wisdom, love, and effort.

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Wisdom (5 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores wisdom as the fifth spiritual faculty, focusing on how it orders the other faculties and how it grows from insight into lived behavior. He distinguishes factual knowledge from the practical knowing of how not to suffer, then moves into how delusion thaws over time, why happiness can broaden receptivity, and why wisdom needs reinforcement through practice and spiritual friendship. The talk closes with a quiet encouragement to keep practicing, keep returning to insight, and try acting from wisdom even when it does not feel fully present.

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties: Samadhi (4 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi as more than narrow concentration, describing it as a gathered, receptive, and internally unified mind. He moves through how samadhi relates to mindfulness, why it depends on letting go rather than force, and how it can support faith and deeper insight. The talk closes by suggesting that learning on a stable mind leaves a deeper mark and lasts longer in the body and practice.
Chapters: 00:09 What Samadhi Is - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=9 01:29 Not Separate Practices - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=89 03:18 Attention and Receptivity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=198 04:53 Aligned Inner Energies - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=293 07:43 Faith Through Stillness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=463 09:32 Letting Go, Not Forcing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=572 11:38 Ground for Insight - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=698 14:16 Closing Encouragement - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17633/?t=856

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Mindfulness (3 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses this short dharmette to unpack what mindfulness is, and just as importantly, what it is not. After noting how the word can get overloaded or diluted, he turns to the Satipatthana Sutta and sketches mindfulness as the opposite of many common features of distress: rumination, fragmentation, avoidance, rigid self-stories, and moralism. The talk stays close to lived experience, ending with mindfulness as a more spacious, stable, less self-centered way of meeting what arises.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the topic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=12 00:46 A loaded word - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=46 02:16 Satipatthana baseline - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=136 03:33 How distress feels - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=213 05:19 What suffering does - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=319 08:38 Less fusion, more space - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=518 10:07 Stability and tolerance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=607 12:29 Less self, more wakefulness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17636/?t=749
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Energy (2 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the energy faculty as a middle way between forceful striving and passivity. He moves from a recent retreat theme of doing and non-doing into a careful look at how willpower can tangle with self-view, then offers a quieter definition of right effort as relaxed perseverance guided by wisdom. The talk closes by connecting habit, craving, and the energy needed to put down suffering’s causes rather than just endure their effects.
Chapters: 00:13 From Faith to Energy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=13 00:32 Doing and Non-Doing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=32 01:30 The Buddha's Relentless Effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=90 02:38 When Effort Becomes Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=158 05:21 Witnessing the Limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=321 07:14 Relaxed Perseverance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=434 08:42 Why Effort Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=522 09:58 Habit and Craving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=598 12:58 Loving the Causes Less - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=778
Source: AudioDharma

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the energy faculty as a middle way between forceful striving and passivity. He moves from a recent retreat theme of doing and non-doing into a careful look at how willpower can tangle with self-view, then offers a quieter definition of right effort as relaxed perseverance guided by wisdom. The talk closes by connecting habit, craving, and the energy needed to put down suffering’s causes rather than just endure their effects.
Chapters: 00:13 From Faith to Energy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=13 00:32 Doing and Non-Doing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=32 01:30 The Buddha's Relentless Effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=90 02:38 When Effort Becomes Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=158 05:21 Witnessing the Limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=321 07:14 Relaxed Perseverance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=434 08:42 Why Effort Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=522 09:58 Habit and Craving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=598 12:58 Loving the Causes Less - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17640/?t=778
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Faith (1 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens a five-part series on the spiritual faculties by focusing on faith, not as belief in fixed ideas, but as a confidence that grows out of suffering, experience, and practice. He moves from the doubt that first makes practice sincere, through scriptural and teaching references, to a steadier trust that feels like love, refuge, and a return to the heart's native ground. The talk closes with faith as a quiet ease that helps us breathe more easily in an unpredictable life.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Theme - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=0 00:45 Faith Begins in Doubt - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=45 01:25 Suffering as Support - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=85 02:40 Not Dogmatic Belief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=160 04:05 Trusting Experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=245 04:50 Faith Like Falling In Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=290 06:32 Its Own Evidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=392 07:38 Stable Yet Living - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=458 09:17 Ease and Consolation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17639/?t=557
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Non-Doing & Liberative Dependent Orignation
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores non-doing as a real trajectory of Dharma practice, moving from effort and courage into a quieter logic of surrender. Using the Therigatha, Augustine, and several contemporary Dharma teachers, he traces how faith, joy, tranquility, concentration, disenchantment, and dispassion can unfold when willfulness relaxes. The talk gradually shifts from the felt work of practice to the possibility that liberation is less something manufactured than something allowed.

item.title: Morning Instruction: Emotions
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a short morning instruction on working with emotions in meditation, using RAIN, breath, and sound to show how feeling can be met without turning it into a prediction about the future. He describes emotion as part of the body’s risk-detecting system, then gently shifts the practice toward opening to intensities, knots, longing, resistance, and dead zones without discrimination. The talk closes by returning to soothing supports and the basic inquiry, "Am I okay?"—not as a verdict on life, but as a practice experiment in direct experience.

item.title: Non-doing: Receptivity & Purification
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver contrasts two instincts on the Dharma path: the drive to manage, improve, and control experience, and the quieter discipline of receptivity. He traces how effort can become tangled with self-view, then turns toward openness, sensitivity, and learning as alternatives to willfulness. In the last part of the talk, he frames difficult phases of practice not as failures but as purification, where grief, pain, and love can all be part of the same release.

item.title: Conversation about Insight
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores insight as something more than analysis: a shift that is freeing, embodied, and often difficult to distinguish from ordinary knowing until it has become real. He contrasts rumination with realization, then brings in examples from Rob Burbea, Nicole Krauss, Jack Kornfield, Ajahn Chah, and others to show how practice changes perception, attention, and the stories we live inside. The conversation later turns to samadhi, motivation, and the way craving and wanting shape what we think we know.

item.title: Whole Presence Samadhi
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver speaks about samadhi as more than concentration: a gathered, easeful, undistracted presence that can become non-separate, embodied, and whole. Using stories of a Zen teacher on a bus, a temple drum, Suzuki Roshi, and his own experience with striving in meditation, he shows how pressure and self-clinging can block the very stillness we want. The talk gradually shifts from defining samadhi to showing how kindness, acceptance, and working gently with hindrances make it available in ordinary life.

item.title: Restlessness, Worry & Compassion
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses the ordinary drama of making oatmeal to open a wider reflection on restlessness, worry, and the habit of trying to control the future. He moves from Buddhist ideas about anicca, dukkha, and bhava tanha into the practical work of settling the mind, meeting uncertainty, and recognizing many alarm bells as false alarms. The talk gradually turns toward compassion: for grief, for regret, and for the fact that freedom asks for love rather than perfection.

item.title: Ill-Will & Metta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.

item.title: Dharmette: The Pleasure of Spiritual Friendship
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the pleasure of spiritual friendship as a quiet but important part of Dharma life. He describes intimacy as the willingness to see and be seen, and argues that good friends help us be known in both our strength and our imperfection. The talk moves from the role of Sangha to the way friendship reorients attention toward goodness, beauty, and the urgency of practice.

item.title: Dharmette: The Pleasure of Purification
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why the difficult parts of practice matter, arguing that the path is not only about peaks of calm or insight but also about the descent, the valley, and the discomfort that can carry the taste of purification. He contrasts the partial pleasure of clinging with the quieter relief that comes from meeting pain with equanimity, surrender, and a willingness to be changed. The talk draws on Ajahn Chah and Shinzen Young to show how wholesome discomfort can become a kind of joy, softness, and freedom.
Chapters: 00:09 Beyond the Peaks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=9 01:25 Two Tastes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=85 02:37 Acquired Taste - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=157 03:59 Clinging's Reward - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=239 06:18 Pain of Healing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=378 07:37 Deep Joy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=457 09:03 Surrender and Exposure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=543 11:19 Freedom Afterward - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=679 13:37 Poignant Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17002/?t=817

item.title: Dharmette: Sourcing our Motivation from Love Rather than Fear
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the difference between motivating ourselves through fear and motivating ourselves through love. Using a story from residential treatment work, he shows how harsh self-management can seem effective at first, but tends not to support lasting change. The talk moves toward a quieter alternative: curiosity, humor, tenderness, and care as the basis for practice and daily life.
Chapters: 00:34 A Different Dharma Pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=34 02:01 Working With Boys - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=121 02:56 Fear or Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=176 04:08 What Drives Us - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=248 05:17 Self-Harshness and Proof - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=317 07:53 Curiosity and Humor - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=473 09:41 Care Over Urgency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=581 12:01 Less Entangled - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17000/?t=721
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharma Pleasure - The Pleasure of Having a Path
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the "pleasure of having a path" as a kind of Dharma pleasure that does not feed craving. He begins with the idea that ordinary pleasures often intensify thirst, then turns to the relief that comes from finding a practice that feels personally trustworthy. The talk moves through faith, purpose, and direction, using brief references to Nietzsche, Vaclav Havel, and Ken McLeod to show how confidence in wisdom and love can steady practice even when life remains unclear.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Seven Factors of Awakening
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the seven factors of awakening as a living structure of practice rather than a dry list. He moves from mindfulness and investigation through energy, rapture, tranquility, samadhi, and equanimity, showing how the factors support one another and mature over time. The talk emphasizes that awakening is both cultivated and discovered: the path and the fruit are not separate.

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as Existential Therapy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a kind of existential therapy: a way of meeting death, impermanence, freedom, isolation, and responsibility without flinching. He moves from stark quotations about mortality into a quiet case for practicing with groundlessness, building meaning from within lived experience, and becoming more ethically responsive to others. The talk stays practical and grounded, with literary and Buddhist references used to clarify the work of waking up.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as Exposure Therapy for Samsara
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a kind of exposure therapy: turning toward the thoughts, feelings, and bodily states we usually avoid. He moves from clinical language about experiential avoidance to the way meditation, especially retreat practice, gradually reveals dukkha and asks for a deeper intimacy with it. The talk closes with a few reflective references that sharpen the point that what is bitter can become nourishment when it is fully met.

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as Cognitive Therapy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a form of cognitive therapy: not just training attention, but changing the views and assumptions that organize experience. He moves from Aaron Beck’s model of schemas to Buddhist wise view, the three characteristics, and a fresh way of seeing the self. The talk ends with a practical note that attentional steadiness helps these new views take deeper root.

item.title: Dharmette: Dharma as an Attention Therapy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a kind of training for attention: a way of recovering agency, reducing fragmentation, and making insight more durable. He moves from the pressures modern life places on attention, through examples of attentional disturbance in depression, anxiety, addiction, and ordinary suffering, to the steadiness of samadhi and how it helps Dharma lessons sink in more deeply. The talk ends by emphasizing that concentration and insight belong together, and that deeper happiness is tied to open-heartedness rather than closed-hearted control.
Chapters: 00:11 Opening the framework - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=11 01:09 Attention as agency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=69 02:04 Modern life and mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=124 03:23 Disturbed attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=203 05:15 Training the attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=315 07:24 Poise and clarity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=444 09:08 Seeing suffering clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=548 10:43 Insights that stick - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16812/?t=643
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: The Mechanisms of Action of the Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as having a “mechanism of action,” borrowing the language of clinical research to ask not only whether the path works, but how it works. He moves through classical Buddhist framings—ethical conduct, collectedness, and wisdom—then adds a promised alternative map for the rest of the week. The talk stays practical and encouraging, with brief references to Rilke, Twain, and Bob Dylan used to underline perseverance, transparency, and refuge.
Chapters: 00:12 Why Ask How It Works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=12 01:11 Practice as Change - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=71 02:57 A Strong Reason to Continue - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=177 04:36 Rilke and Perseverance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=276 05:39 Ethics and Non-Regret - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=339 07:12 Truth and Shelter - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=432 09:16 Collected Attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=556 11:28 A New Map Ahead - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=688
Source: AudioDharma

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as having a “mechanism of action,” borrowing the language of clinical research to ask not only whether the path works, but how it works. He moves through classical Buddhist framings—ethical conduct, collectedness, and wisdom—then adds a promised alternative map for the rest of the week. The talk stays practical and encouraging, with brief references to Rilke, Twain, and Bob Dylan used to underline perseverance, transparency, and refuge.
Chapters: 00:12 Why Ask How It Works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=12 01:11 Practice as Change - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=71 02:57 A Strong Reason to Continue - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=177 04:36 Rilke and Perseverance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=276 05:39 Ethics and Non-Regret - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=339 07:12 Truth and Shelter - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=432 09:16 Collected Attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=556 11:28 A New Map Ahead - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16814/?t=688
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16814

item.title: Dharma and Concern for the World
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what it means to care about the world without collapsing into despair, numbness, or constant reaction. He moves from the strain of news and social grief into a Dharma-centered inquiry about suffering, attention, action, and rest, with brief references to Justin Rosenstein, Tom Waits, Toby Ord, and others. The talk keeps returning to a practical question: how to engage the world honestly, without wasting our limited energy or losing our heart.
Chapters: 00:12 Dharma as inquiry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=12 02:54 Extraction and attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=174 05:18 Collapse and helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=318 10:09 Holding on - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=609 12:01 What to do - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=721 16:00 Seeing what improves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=960 21:01 A risky century - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=1261 25:40 Dharma and other medicines - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=1540 31:11 Give, rest, and let go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16544/?t=1871

item.title: Developing our Dharma Stories
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how Dharma practice works through both direct experience and the stories we tell ourselves. He moves from the use of thought as a temporary support, to the importance of personal, well-fitted “Dharma stories,” and then into a sustained meditation on mortality, helplessness, and the way death-awareness can loosen fear and self-clinging. The talk closes by shifting from getting to learning, and from self-improvement toward a life that can meet whatever arises as practice.

item.title: Monday Dharma Talk: Relationship between the Body and Thinking
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how bodily affect and thinking shape one another, arguing that insight comes less from thinking our way through experience than from gathering data in awareness, especially through the body. He moves from basic Buddhist framing to a close look at valence, neutrality, prediction, and the stories the mind builds around feeling. The talk gradually becomes practical: how to recognize false alarms, meet unpleasantness without narrative inflation, and let bodily signals inform wiser action.

item.title: Sunday Evening Reflection
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a Zen paradox: how a person can be entirely worthy of love and, at the same time, committed to real change. He turns that tension into a practical invitation—drop spiritual bargaining, accept what has come to be, and let the wish for growth arise without desperation. The talk closes by bringing the teaching into retreat life: rest, notice the good that has been cultivated, and simply proceed as feels fitting.

item.title: Dharmette on Retreat Theme: Thought & Freedom
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the retreat theme of thought and freedom by first grounding practice in ordinary life, with its habits, messiness, and unedited experience. He then turns to the way thought can both trap and serve us, arguing that mindfulness is not the enemy of thinking but a flexible way of knowing when to make meaning and when to let meaning thin out. The talk closes with a note of confidence: the Dharma can sink deeply into the mind so that goodness becomes an inward, steady support.

item.title: Love as the Default Position of the Mind
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores love as the mind’s natural resting place, moving from the tension between acceptance and change to the way practice can free up tenderness rather than manufacture it. He draws on Bohr, Suzuki Roshi, Maslow, Levinas, Joseph Goldstein, Thich Nhat Hanh, and others to show how insight into impermanence, not-self, and suffering can deepen love. The talk settles into a quiet argument that practice is less about fixing the self than loosening clinging so attention, care, and equanimity become more available.

item.title: No escape other than into the heart (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a retreat talk about why silence can feel more real than ordinary life: it reveals how mind shapes experience and how suffering is often personalized into shame. He moves from the hindrances to the First Noble Truth, then toward a practical instruction to stay close to feeling, let the heart be impacted, and use awareness and love to transform difficulty rather than escape it. The talk includes references to the Dhammapada, Ajahn Chah, Thich Nhat Hanh, and Shinzen Young, all supporting the same central shift: no escape except into the heart.
Chapters: 00:11 The hindrances in retreat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=11 02:14 Retreat as real life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=134 04:47 Mind shapes experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=287 07:16 Conditioning and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=436 12:33 Nourishing what arises - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=753 18:51 The heart begins the path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=1131 21:48 Pain is not enough - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=1308 31:42 Greed, hatred, delusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=1902 39:16 Stay with the body - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-70711/?t=2356

item.title: Reflections on Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on equanimity as a form of rest for the heart: not indifference, but the capacity to meet experience without constant strain. He moves from a brief Ajahn Buddhadasa line through the tension between caring and not caring, then into the balance of equanimity and compassion in the face of loss and grief. The talk stays grounded in ordinary exhaustion, impermanence, and the way a steadier heart can serve more wisely.

item.title: Sympathetic Joy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores mudita, or sympathetic joy, as something more relational and less forced than ordinary happiness. He begins with the difficulty of “being joyful,” then uses a quoted teaching from a colleague to show joy as something we can receive through other people’s well-being, even alongside grief. The talk closes by turning toward the main obstacles to mudita—comparison, envy, possessiveness, and egoic identification—and by asking what it means to let another person’s happiness be unthreatening.
Chapters: 00:14 What mudita is - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=14 01:30 A guide’s example - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=90 02:15 Joy through connection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=135 05:22 Joy and grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=322 06:42 Removing obstacles - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=402 07:17 Envy and ego - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=437 10:13 Against the stream - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=613 13:09 Nothing is taken - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16264/?t=789

item.title: Reflections on Compassion
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on compassion as the natural response to dukkha, starting with the shock of suffering itself and moving toward the need to understand suffering more deeply. He contrasts empathy with compassion, drawing on a research example with Matthieu Ricard and Tanya Singer, and then turns to a quieter view of love that is not enmeshment, control, or emotional contagion. The talk closes with a practical invitation to let care for others become more wholehearted and less contracted around our own pain.
Chapters: 00:13 The shock of suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=13 02:17 Comprehending dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=137 03:57 Love instead of control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=237 06:21 Diagnosing our pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=381 09:12 Compassion and empathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=552 11:30 True love doesn't hurt - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=690 13:33 A wholehearted wish - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16259/?t=813
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Reflections on Lovingkindness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on lovingkindness as a practice that should feel real rather than performative, opening with a line from Carol Wilson about “fake metta” and then turning to the way fear and wanting are intertwined. He moves between Dharma language, a brief neuroscience aside, and lived examples of family attachment to show how metta can soften anxiety, meet pain, and reveal goodness. The talk settles into metta as both cultivation and purification: a warmth that does not control, a way of approaching what is unresolved, and a practice that makes hatred less tenable.
Chapters: 00:12 Sincere practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=12 01:34 Fear and wanting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=94 04:13 Fear’s force - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=253 05:18 Approaching anxiety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=318 06:29 Metta for fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=389 08:12 Attachment and care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=492 09:56 Seeing goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=596 11:37 Moved by goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=697 13:25 Purification and blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16266/?t=805

item.title: Orienting to the Divine Abodes
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with Maslow’s idea that self-actualization can be a transition beyond identity, then uses that lens to suggest that practice points toward love rather than self-enclosure. He reframes the Buddhist path as a natural resting place for attention: the Brahma-viharas as modes of loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity that meet different conditions without clinging. The talk closes with a teaching story about a practitioner who, after years of effort, suddenly feels self-kindness directly for herself.
Chapters: 00:13 Maslow and self-erasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=13 01:46 Love feels natural - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=106 03:01 Beyond state chasing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=181 04:34 Where attention lives - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=274 07:00 The divine abodes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=420 09:39 Clinging and peace - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=579 11:06 Love in every condition - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=666 13:03 A story of felt metta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16256/?t=783

item.title: Samvega and Love of the World
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores a central Buddhist tension: disenchantment with samsara and real love for the world. Beginning with a short passage about crossing the flood without pushing forward or staying in place, he uses that paradox to frame practice as holding opposites without collapsing into either extreme. The talk moves through renunciation, samvega, and the question of what it means to care deeply for suffering beings while not making a home in fleeting conditions.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Meaning And Nonconceptualization
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the tension between the stories we live by and the mind’s drive to make meaning. He suggests that Dharma can use thought skillfully, then asks what happens when the need to interpret experience softens and the field of experience becomes more intimate and spacious. The talk moves from fear-based meaning-making to a quieter, less entangled way of knowing, ending with the idea that a life can become a Dharma story shaped by release, love, and space.

item.title: Grief and Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on grief as a natural part of loving in a changing world, beginning with his father’s death and widening to history, ecology, and ordinary loss. He then turns toward spiritual practice, arguing that maturation, ethical growth, and deepening compassion often carry a grief-like feeling as we loosen grasping and expand our care. The talk closes with a quiet case for making room for grief rather than treating it as a problem to be eliminated.
Chapters: 00:12 Loss Reveals the World - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=12 02:56 Why Grief Belongs - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=176 04:21 Growing Up Spiritually - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=261 05:48 Learning Better - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=348 07:11 Love Meets Suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=431 08:34 Permission to Grieve - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=514 10:24 Beyond Yet Not Without - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=624 12:13 A Deeper, Truer Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16078/?t=733
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Renunciation and Enjoying Pleasure
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on renunciation as a practical part of Dharma rather than a grim denial of life. Using a William James quote and a brief reference to Zadig Amaro, he argues that practice trains the nervous system, loosens craving, and can make room for a cleaner, less compulsive enjoyment of pleasure. The talk stays close to the tension between wanting comfort and offering oneself to the path, then ends by framing relinquishment as something that frees us rather than impoverishes us.
Chapters: 00:12 Habit and training - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=12 01:11 Renunciation and resistance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=71 03:12 A central practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=192 04:40 Letting go eases fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=280 05:31 Pleasure without defense - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=331 07:32 Craving and desire - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=452 09:11 Equanimity with pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=551 11:00 De-shaming and freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16075/?t=660
Source: AudioDharma
