item.title: Helplessness and Power
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames the talk around two teachings that can seem opposed but actually support each other: helplessness and power. He begins by pairing difficult Dharma themes with their stabilizing counterparts, then explores how recognizing our limits can soften self-hatred, reduce the urge to control everything, and make room for a more honest kind of agency. The talk closes by connecting this insight to practice, ethical conduct, and the widening responsibility that comes with real power.
Chapters: 00:16 Nothing Dominates Everything - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=16 00:54 Pairing the Teachings - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=54 02:16 The Reality of Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=136 05:01 Control and Self-Blame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=301 06:38 Opening to Surrender - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=398 09:12 Finding Wise Power - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=552 11:12 Practice Increases Impact - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=672 13:18 Closing Reflections - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16077/?t=798
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Reflections on Thich Nhat Hanh's Teachings
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on Thich Nhat Hanh as a teacher whose deepest transmission was not authority but love. He moves from personal remembrance and early encounters with Thich Nhat Hanh’s community into themes of suffering, compassion, anger, interbeing, and fearlessness, using memorable images like “no mud, no lotus” and the cloud in the paper. The talk closes with a tender story of renunciation and asks what it means to receive and carry forward Thich Nhat Hanh’s love.

item.title: Mindfulness, Dharma, Mental Health & Science Part 2 of 2
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how mindfulness and Dharma overlap with clinical ideas like attention training, cognitive therapy, exposure, and existential work, while insisting that the path is larger than any single technique. He keeps returning to dependent origination, the reduction of shame, and the way practice loosens avoidance and self-reification over time. In the final section, the talk opens into questions from the room about trauma, culture, sincerity, and how Dharma and mental health can genuinely inform each other.
Chapters: 00:12 Psychiatric complexity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=12 03:07 Causality as dependence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=187 06:08 Dharma as multiple methods - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=368 09:16 Attention and right view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=556 14:39 Thoughts, depression, and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=879 19:58 Exposure and approach - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=1198 29:10 Tranquility and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=1750 37:54 Equanimity, self, and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=2274 59:18 Questions and integration - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16025/?t=3558

item.title: Mindfulness, Dharma, Mental Health & Science Part 1 of 2
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how mindfulness has moved between Dharma, science, medicine, and mental health, without treating any one frame as the whole story. He looks at the growth of meditation research, the public-health use of mindfulness, and the question of what gets preserved or lost when contemplative practice enters clinical and secular settings. The talk moves from William James and Maslow to Marsha Linehan and Jon Kabat-Zinn, then opens into a discussion of attrition, approach orientation, and whether reverence for the sacred can coexist with empiricism.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=12 01:00 James and attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=60 03:27 Meditation in public life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=207 08:52 Well-being and dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=532 11:40 Dharma and science - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=700 15:55 Ways of relating - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=955 25:10 Linehan as example - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=1510 30:38 Mindfulness programs - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=1838 41:00 Questions and tensions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-16024/?t=2460
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Love and its relation with wisdom.
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how love and wisdom are not separate aims but mutually supporting wings of practice. He moves from examples of moral credibility and admired teachers into his own account of sensitivity, cynicism, and learning to trust that change is only really possible in the presence of love. The talk broadens from personal practice to sangha, ethics, and even long-term human responsibility, ending with a quiet question about how we will spend the love we cultivate.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and intention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=0 01:31 Love needs credibility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=91 04:02 Practice as art - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=242 07:10 Finite life, love's necessity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=430 11:40 From suspicion to trust - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=700 15:50 Recognizing goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=950 19:40 Dharma love and the wings - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=1180 26:42 The river of compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=1602 29:03 Love in every stage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69772/?t=1743
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Memory, prediction, Bharatanha, and the present
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how the mind becomes caught in memory and prediction, and how that catch shapes our sense of self, time, and safety. He moves from the felt experience of boredom, pressure, and unfinished life into a quieter argument: practice is not about forcing attention into the present, but about becoming available to it through awareness, love, and a more purified view. Along the way he draws on Thich Nhat Hanh, Elie Wiesel, Buddhist teachings on the three characteristics, and a brief neuroscience reference to show how past and future are woven together.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the room - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=0 02:15 Caught in time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=135 05:50 Availability, not force - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=350 08:00 Memory and the past - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=480 13:11 Rewriting the story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=791 20:13 Digesting experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=1213 26:32 Prediction and survival - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=1592 31:22 Becoming and fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=1882 37:22 Letting the future go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-69750/?t=2242

item.title: Mindfulness, Thinking, Mindlessness, Rumination
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver looks at thinking as something more varied than a simple enemy of mindfulness. He distinguishes ordinary mindlessness, harmless going-slack, and the more corrosive patterns of rumination, using both Dharma framing and cognitive-science references to show how attention can collapse into thought and then recover. The talk stays practical throughout, returning again and again to the body, to feeling, and to the question of how much attentional energy a day can really hold.

item.title: Affect and the Interpersonal Realm (5 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how feeling shapes attention, meaning, and action, and then brings that view into the interpersonal realm: intimacy, shame, pleasure, ambivalence, and the courage to admit when we are wrong. He argues that emotional wisdom is not mainly about control, but about meeting feeling with clarity, tenderness, and skill. In the closing section, he turns to the body as a stabilizer and offers simple relaxation instructions as a way to let charged energy discharge and soften suffering.
Chapters: 00:12 Feeling shapes mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=12 02:58 Feeling at the center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=178 04:58 Threat and opportunity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=298 06:50 Love and vulnerability - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=410 08:53 Equanimity with pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=533 11:25 Being wrong gently - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=685 13:05 Body as stabilizer - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=785 15:05 Empathy deepens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14569/?t=905

item.title: Strength and the Surrender to Goodness (4 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on reverence for another person’s goodness, arguing that healthy idealization can be part of love, relationship, and Dharma practice. He distinguishes reverence from projection or dependency, and suggests that surrender can be a form of strength rather than weakness. The talk closes by linking metta to the capacity to see goodness without turning it into comparison, envy, or self-judgment.
Chapters: 01:00 From pain to goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=60 02:19 Why surrender matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=139 03:36 Idealization and illusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=216 05:07 Reverence, not projection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=307 06:34 Surrender to Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=394 08:02 Awe for goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=482 09:52 Envy and comparison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=592 11:11 Metta and surrender - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=671 12:19 Closing reflections - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14571/?t=739

item.title: The Pain of the Other Spins Our Mind (3 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores why the suffering of people close to us can affect the mind so powerfully, sometimes more than our own pain. He moves from the first noble truth into the way love, clinging, family history, and identification make another person's dukkha feel personal and destabilizing. The talk ends by distinguishing compassion from the wish to control, and by asking what in us is actually evoked when we face the imperfections of someone we love.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=12 01:05 Personal Suffering Realized - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=65 02:02 The Suffering of Others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=122 03:11 Love and Clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=191 05:25 One Mind, One Robe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=325 07:18 When Reactivity Takes Over - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=438 09:48 Change Needs Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=588 12:30 Compassion and Forgiveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=750 14:01 A Treaty of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=841

item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver explores why the suffering of people close to us can affect the mind so powerfully, sometimes more than our own pain. He moves from the first noble truth into the way love, clinging, family history, and identification make another person's dukkha feel personal and destabilizing. The talk ends by distinguishing compassion from the wish to control, and by asking what in us is actually evoked when we face the imperfections of someone we love.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=12 01:05 Personal Suffering Realized - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=65 02:02 The Suffering of Others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=122 03:11 Love and Clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=191 05:25 One Mind, One Robe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=325 07:18 When Reactivity Takes Over - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=438 09:48 Change Needs Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=588 12:30 Compassion and Forgiveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=750 14:01 A Treaty of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14561/?t=841
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14561

item.title: Not-knowing is the Most Intimate (2 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how self-understanding and intimacy change when we stop forcing fixed answers onto identity. Drawing on Simone Weil, a Zen story about “not knowing,” Philip Roth, and a brief father-daughter exchange, he argues that uncertainty can soften self-judgment and make relationships feel more alive. The talk moves from the question “Who am I?” to a quieter reverence for the mystery of other people.
Chapters: 00:23 The question of self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=23 02:04 When autobiography cracks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=124 03:31 We are more than imagined - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=211 04:39 Not knowing is intimate - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=279 05:15 Seeing loved ones anew - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=315 07:22 The hidden life of others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=442 08:53 Roles and predictability - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=533 11:09 Beyond the role - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=669 13:00 Looking with new eyes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14559/?t=780
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Interpersonal Life as Dharma Practice (1 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames interpersonal life as a central field of Dharma practice, starting with the reminder that the self is not isolated and that relationship reveals our habits plainly. He moves from humor and vulnerability into a serious reflection on belonging, nervous system regulation, and the difference between knowing the Dharma and actually realizing it in lived experience. The talk ends by inviting a week of careful, forgiving inquiry into what gets activated in us with others, and how the Dharma feels in relationship compared with solitude.

item.title: Love and Its Relation to Wisdom
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how love and wisdom support each other, beginning with personal stories and literary references and moving toward a more practical Dharma question: how does a heart become large enough to meet suffering without closing down? He argues that love is not separate from clear seeing, and that understanding, attunement, and reverence are part of how practice matures. In the last part of the talk, he widens the frame to collective suffering, modern risk, and the limits of expecting Dharma practice to do everything at once.

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Phillip Moffitt, Tuere Sala: Dialogue on aspects of freedom.
item.description:
This dialogue explores freedom as something with both depth and breadth: moments of insight, and the slower way practice reshapes ordinary life. Matthew Brensilver, Philip Moffitt, and Tuere Sala reflect on non-self, habit energy, purification of motive, and the way love and compassion mature through contact with dukkha. The talk moves from humorous banter into a grounded discussion of striving, gradual change, and why practice continues even when nothing dramatic seems to be happening.

item.title: Tranquilizing side of the 7 Factors: Agitation and temporal density.
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the tranquilizing side of the seven factors of awakening, moving from the charged beauty of loving-kindness into the quieter liberation of peace, samadhi, and equanimity. He argues that agitation thins and time feels less dense when the mind stops compulsively predicting, interpreting, and securing itself. The talk blends personal story, poetry, and references to several teachers to show why letting go can feel less like producing a state and more like surrendering into rest.
Chapters: 00:30 Kindness Overflowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=30 04:44 Why Peace Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=284 09:43 Road Before the Bend - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=583 12:13 Agitation And Time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=733 20:11 Animal Vigilance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=1211 27:20 Equanimity Softens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=1640 32:47 Samadhi As Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=1967 40:34 Settling Into Refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-67330/?t=2434

item.title: Equanimity (5 of 5):
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver closes a five-part series on equanimity by correcting a common misunderstanding: equanimity is not indifference, cynicism, or passivity. He moves from a playful opening to a careful definition of non-interference, then shows how equanimity can deepen compassion without becoming overwhelmed by suffering. The talk ends with a wider ethical horizon, including a brief Derek Parfit citation about reasons to be glad the universe exists.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening reflection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=12 01:20 Not cynicism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=80 03:04 What equanimity is - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=184 05:32 Heart with force - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=332 06:53 Middle path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=413 08:46 Compassion purified - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=526 12:04 Rest and renew - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=724 14:23 Closing thanks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14009/?t=863
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Equanimity (4 of 5): Skillfully Meeting Feelings Associated with Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how identity is often built through subtle performances: the ways we present, defend, and curate a self. He moves from social masks and shame into Buddhist practice, showing how afflictive feelings reveal self-view and how equanimity lets those identifications soften without turning the ego into an enemy. The talk closes with a gentler tone: selfing can be met with love, allowing life to touch us rather than trying to shape life around a fixed image of who we are.
Chapters: 00:12 Planting a Flag - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=12 01:25 Life as Performance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=85 03:12 The Self as Argument - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=192 04:53 Pain Reveals Self-View - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=293 07:23 Unconvinced by Story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=443 09:01 Meeting with Equanimity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=541 10:30 Grief and Nonself - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=630 12:24 Love Instead of Defense - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-14000/?t=744
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Equanimity (3 of 5): Transmuting Dukkha
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores equanimity as a way of meeting pain without wasting it. He contrasts this with self-denial and spiritual hardness, drawing on the Buddha’s middle path, Ajahn Sumedho, Thich Nhat Hanh, and Shinzen Young to show how difficulty can mature into love and a longing for liberation. The talk stays close to practice: soften, stay, and let dukkha become part of awakening rather than a source of more harm.
Chapters: 00:14 Closeness Across Distance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=14 01:18 The Middle Path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=78 02:46 Grasping and Desire - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=166 03:46 The Limits of Austerity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=226 05:02 Not Squandering Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=302 06:32 Love Through Difficulty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=392 07:34 Softening to Conditions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=454 09:12 Pain Met Wisely - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=552 11:54 Catharsis and Aftereffects - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13995/?t=714

item.title: Equanimity (2 of 5): Safety and Compulsive Hypothesis-Testing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores equanimity as a way of meeting the body’s deep concern for safety without turning against ourselves. Drawing on a neuroscience model of the brain as a hypothesis-testing organ, he shows how fear, pain, and the pull toward prediction can narrow experience and make even small discomforts feel urgent. The talk gradually moves from analysis into practice, ending with a quieter sense of refuge, openness, and trust in the path.

item.title: Equanimity (1 of 5): Introduction
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens a week on equanimity by situating it as a practical foundation for the path, especially in relation to non-clinging and the root defilements. He moves from a broad description of wise attention into several working definitions of equanimity: inner balance, non-compulsion, widened perspective, and the space to meet difficulty without laundering it into a story. The talk closes by showing how equanimity can be developed with greed, aversion, and delusion, so that the heart does not have to keep defending or justifying them.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening and context - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=12 01:19 Attention and wisdom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=79 02:44 Equanimity and non-clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=164 05:00 Widening perspective - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=300 06:16 Balance and realism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=376 07:32 Opening to dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=452 09:41 Why it matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=581 11:47 Working with the defilements - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-13983/?t=707
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Anatta and its Blessings
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores anatta not as a new philosophy to adopt, but as a way of loosening the habits that turn experience into a fixed self. He moves from grief and confusion around self-view to the relief, tenderness, and connection that can follow when clinging softens. Along the way he uses examples from close relationships, self-esteem, envy, and identity to show how selfing shapes suffering and how its fading can open intimacy.
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=0 03:07 Grief before relief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=187 09:06 What not-self means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=546 14:18 Looking for the self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=858 20:58 Self-love and defensiveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=1258 28:00 Narrative gravity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=1680 35:49 Freedom from comparison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=2149 42:39 Open air - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-68411/?t=2559
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Sunday Closing Dharmette
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on awakening as more than a single breakthrough, arguing for the importance of gradual cultivation and the "breadth" of practice in ordinary conduct. He then turns to sila as the way the path becomes a refuge for others, linking ethics with grief, love, and the ongoing work of softening the heart. The talk closes with a gentle invitation to notice what the stillness asks of one’s life, followed by brief closing announcements and teacher reflections on imperfection and gratitude.

item.title: Saturday Dharma Talk: Freedom to Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores freedom as something that is not only freedom from clinging, but freedom to love. He moves from the Dharma’s map of suffering into a quieter argument that real love is spacious, wise, grief-informed, and not confused with control, sentimentality, or avoidance. Along the way he draws on Tolstoy, Thich Nhat Hanh, the Dalai Lama, Gandhi, Parfit, and a teaching story about dāna to show how practice can soften defensiveness and widen the heart.

item.title: Thursday Dharma Talk: Not-Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver approaches not-self as a lived investigation rather than a philosophy to settle. He moves from the habit of making identity feel solid, through self-acceptance and the thinning of self-view, toward a more spacious kind of knowing that is still ethical and caring. Along the way he warns against spiritual bypassing, uses examples from research and contemporary teachers, and returns several times to the simple question of what clinging is doing in the moment.
Chapters: 00:40 Why simplicity misleads - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=40 02:54 Not-self as practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=174 06:22 Beyond views and fantasies - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=382 10:10 Misreading the teaching - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=610 16:59 Self-love and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=1019 21:53 Self-view and conceit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=1313 32:27 Narrative gravity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=1947 39:59 Fear and safety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=2399 45:58 Empty center, open heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12555/?t=2758

item.title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a line from Rohini’s enlightenment poem: the path is not a line on a map, but a great shining world, enter wherever you like. He uses that image to gently undo the pressure to fit Dharma practice into a fixed shape, suggesting instead that we discover our own belonging within it. The talk closes with an encouragement to affirm one’s efforts, rest well, and continue noticing how apparent walls may become doorways.

item.title: Tuesday Dharma Talk: Practicing Freely
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what it means to practice freely: not by trying to force freedom, but by noticing the clinging, habits, and assumptions that shape the moment. He moves from a retreat-hall story about competing for the best seat into a wider inquiry about intrinsic motivation, humility, awe, and surrender. The talk gradually turns toward difficult practice, the value of wholesome discomfort, and the possibility that the Dharma can feel less like something we choose and more like something that chooses us.
Chapters: 00:24 Retreat Hall Clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=24 02:15 How Practice Reveals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=135 09:12 Background Mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=552 11:54 Ladders and Imperfection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=714 15:01 Intrinsic Motivation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=901 23:54 Humility and Awe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=1434 28:01 Curiosity Over Discipline - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=1681 31:51 Self-Review and Purification - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=1911 41:03 For Others and the Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12537/?t=2463

item.title: Monday Evening Reflection: A little bit freer
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the difference between wanting freedom and practicing freedom, especially when practice feels ordinary, difficult, or unrewarding. He normalizes the discouragement that can come early in retreat and suggests looking for very small shifts rather than grand breakthroughs. The talk closes with Suzuki Roshi’s image of getting wet in the mist, a reminder that freedom often arrives gradually and almost invisibly.

item.title: Improvisation and the Messiness of Experience
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on Dharma practice as something lived in the middle of confusion, improvisation, and not knowing. Using The Big Lebowski, retreat guidance, and several quoted teachers, he contrasts polished instructions with the actual mess of experience and the ways practitioners can abandon themselves by pretending to be more composed than they are. The talk slowly moves toward faith, helplessness, kindness, and the possibility that difficulty itself can open into wisdom and compassion.
Chapters: 00:00 A Complicated Case - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=0 01:47 Cliff Notes and Novels - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=107 04:42 Improvising in Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=282 10:20 Listening Without Distortion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=620 13:32 Trusting Guidance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=812 19:56 Not Knowing, Resting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=1196 26:16 Monastic Roots, Lay Questions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=1576 30:46 Faith at the Stuck Place - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=1846 36:48 Helplessness and Compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12261/?t=2208

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Tuere Sala: Tuere Sala and Matthew Brensilver in dialogue about wisdom
item.description:
This dialogue turns on a quiet question: what is wisdom, really, and how does it mature in practice? Tuere Sala and Matthew Brensilver move from an opening poem about trees as listeners into a grounded conversation about generosity, retreat practice, suffering, and the way insight becomes lived understanding rather than theory. They close by returning to poetry and to the need for clear signals, mutual recognition, and wakefulness in relationship.

item.title: The Parami of Truthfulness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores truthfulness as a Dharma quality: not a claim to final truth, but a steady fidelity to experience, reality, and speech. He moves from scripture and Buddhist psychology into the ways the mind rationalizes, hides, and protects itself, then turns toward honesty as a practice of courage, transparency, and humility. The talk closes by connecting truthful speech with freedom for both oneself and others, and with a gentler way of moving through uncertainty.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=0 01:32 Truth and humility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=92 06:12 Fidelity to reality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=372 09:36 Courage to see - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=576 15:57 Against the current - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=957 25:25 Refining the view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=1525 31:18 Secrets and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=1878 38:19 Truthful speech - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=2299 42:18 Humility and closing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-63035/?t=2538

item.title: Reflections on Truth and Honesty
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on truth, honesty, and the ways self-deception quietly shapes practice and daily life. He moves from grief and the many-faceted reality of the moment into a more grounded claim: we may not know capital-T Truth, but we can learn to value honesty, curiosity, and a willingness to be wrong. The talk then turns to secrecy, shame, and the ethical force of speaking and seeing clearly, for oneself and for others.

item.title: Preparing Citta not to Burn - IRC mini-retreat
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on dukkha as layered and unavoidable, beginning from climate grief, fragility, and a William James image of people skating on a melting lake. He then moves into gratitude, adaptation in Dharma teaching during COVID and civil unrest, and a careful distinction between trying to put out fires and preparing the heart so it is less likely to ignite. The talk closes by emphasizing that practice may not remove every painful event, but it can cultivate a steadier citta and a surplus of well-being that gets spent in ethical action.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with Dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=0 02:18 Frozen Lake Image - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=138 04:35 Ordinary Gratitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=275 06:28 Balancing the Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=388 07:51 Tradition and Adaptation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=471 11:07 Teaching with Caution - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=667 15:10 Not Putting Out Every Fire - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=910 19:03 Preparing Citta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12019/?t=1143
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Taking the Backwards Ethical Step
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores ethics as a living, lifelong practice rather than a fixed moral identity. Drawing on Buddhist teachings, moral psychology, and recent social grief, he argues that real maturation often includes discomfort, vulnerability, and a willingness to let our sense of self be reworked by suffering and responsibility. The talk moves from traditional safeguards like hiri and ottappa to a broader call to respond to injustice with less rationalization and more courage.
Chapters: 00:10 Teaching Style and Theme - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=10 02:08 Wholesome Unpleasant - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=128 06:15 Ethics as One Fruit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=375 09:08 From Avoiding Harm - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=548 13:42 The Backward Step - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=822 16:33 Moralization and Resistance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=993 24:24 Sensitivity and Vulnerability - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=1464 29:45 Feeling, Renunciation, Courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=1785 36:09 In Place of the Friend - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-11572/?t=2169

item.title: Your Face: The Gaze, Self-view, Intimacy and Letting Go (Online Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how face-to-face contact, even through Zoom, reveals self-view, intimacy, and the ways we project onto one another. Moving from ethics and gaze to meditation, he shows how clinging contracts connection, while loosening the self can make relationships safer, clearer, and more loving. The talk closes with Levinas, a brief reflection on not-self, and a long film example from Her to illustrate unpossessible love.

item.title: Vulnerability, Porousness, Equanimity & Love (Online Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with the feeling of life as wilderness: a place where refuge, alertness, and care matter because we are already exposed. From there he turns to dukkha, the sense of being out of step with our own lives, and then into a sustained inquiry into vulnerability, porousness, and what kind of freedom practice actually offers. He places modern themes like self-compassion and trauma alongside classical renunciation, then settles on equanimity as a way of meeting pain without tightening into fear, melodrama, or hate.
Chapters: 00:01 Wilderness and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=1 02:18 The unlived life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=138 06:04 Pain as the teacher - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=364 08:10 Vulnerability in practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=490 12:43 Fragility and animality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=763 21:36 Classical release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=1296 26:00 Clinging and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=1560 32:00 Equanimity and love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=1920 36:39 Wide perspective, quiet heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61761/?t=2199

item.title: Dialectics of Practice - Opening and Protection (Online Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens this online retreat talk by framing practice as a middle way not only between self-indulgence and self-mortification, but also between wholesome opposites like effort and rest, compassion and equanimity. He then turns to the felt conditions that let practice deepen: grief that can open the heart, lightness that loosens ego, and the need for refuge before insight. The talk closes with a practical theme of opening and protection—how to let the world in without becoming brittle or overwhelmed.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver opens this online retreat talk by framing practice as a middle way not only between self-indulgence and self-mortification, but also between wholesome opposites like effort and rest, compassion and equanimity. He then turns to the felt conditions that let practice deepen: grief that can open the heart, lightness that loosens ego, and the need for refuge before insight. The talk closes with a practical theme of opening and protection—how to let the world in without becoming brittle or overwhelmed.

item.title: Addressing Coronavirus with a Dharma View- Monday Night Dharma Talk
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet Dharma response to the coronavirus moment, balancing public health realism with care for fear, uncertainty, and the mind’s tendency to overreach. He argues that Dharma has a real scope, but not every crisis is best met by Dharma alone; science, medicine, and ordinary acts of care matter deeply too. From there he turns toward refuge, metta, interdependence, and a more radical letting go, asking what kind of heart can remain open without denying the seriousness of the situation.
Chapters: 00:09 Opening the frame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=9 01:28 Dharma and medicine - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=88 04:27 Practice in the moment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=267 09:32 Fear and worry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=572 16:22 Caring for now - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=982 20:13 Resilience and friendship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=1213 28:01 Interdependence and letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=1681 34:39 Radical hearts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=2079 46:24 Closing blessings - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-61133/?t=2784

item.title: Death and the Poignancy of Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on death not as a grim abstraction but as a way to clarify what matters, soften the heart, and loosen the pressure to build a story around the self. He moves through examples from literature, Buddhist teachings, psychology, and personal memory to show how mortality can either harden us into fear or open us toward grief, love, and responsibility. The talk ends by returning to practice as a kind of training in letting go and in making friendship with the mind.
Chapters: 00:23 Opening the topic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=23 03:07 Writing and dancing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=187 10:03 Birth and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=603 16:55 Practicing with death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1015 25:30 Resisting and meaning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1530 29:40 Finitude and grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1780 36:54 Loss makes life vivid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=2214 41:27 Fear, identity, and softness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=2487 50:53 Becoming and letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=3053

item.title: How Goodness Accrues on the Path
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how goodness accumulates on the path by way of practice, teaching, and repeated ethical attention. He moves from the Buddha’s instruction to investigate suffering toward a quieter argument that real change is often gradual, idiosyncratic, and less centered on self-improvement than we expect. Along the way he brings in Suzuki Roshi, Rumi, Ajahn Sumedho, Jack Kornfield, and research on happiness to show how Dharma practice can steady the heart, soften self-concern, and deepen moral clarity.

item.title: Attention, Wisdom, Grief and Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses a series of teaching stories, literary references, and Buddhist images to explore how attention, wisdom, grief, and love belong together. He moves from finitude and responsibility into the claim that love is paying attention, then into a deeper argument that letting go often feels like grief before it feels like freedom. By the end, the talk turns toward non-ownership, Dana as offering, and the sense that insight naturally shows itself as love.

item.title: Loving the Self to Death
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses a story about a child falling in the rain to explore the thin line between ordinary difficulty and self-judgment. From there he unfolds a practice of meeting envy, confusion, memory, and even the sense of self with steadier love rather than inner warfare. The talk moves toward a quiet claim: that practice may soften us not by fixing the self, but by loving experience so completely that the sense of a separate self loosens.

item.title: Brian Lesage, Matthew Brensilver: The Interpersonal Dimension of the Dharma (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver and Brian Lesage explore how Dharma practice changes when it enters relationship: speech, listening, clinging, praise, criticism, and the wish to be seen clearly. The talk moves from the simple fact that other people reveal our habits of mind, into a more careful look at wise speech and the tension between ethical aspiration and perfectionism. Near the end, the discussion widens from personal conduct to the question of what a good life asks of us in a world of suffering.

item.title: The Story of Jumping Mouse (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses the story of Jumping Mouse as a frame for an evening retreat talk on the spiritual path: hearing a distant call, leaving comfort, and learning to trust disorientation. He moves through the mouse’s encounters with raccoon, frog, buffalo, wolf, and eagle to explore curiosity, humility, renunciation, refuge, and surrender. The talk also draws on other teachers and texts to show how practice can exceed what we thought we were signing up for.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=0 01:51 Why stories matter - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=111 06:28 Hearing the river - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=388 11:40 Walking with raccoon - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=700 18:10 River and mountain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=1090 30:19 Choosing the wider path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=1819 40:49 Views and surrender - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=2449 54:39 Refuge and closing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56979/?t=3279

item.title: Equanimity and Samadhi
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how equanimity and samadhi grow from letting go rather than from effortful control. He moves from the Buddha’s language of clinging and non-clinging into a practical discussion of worry, self-referential thought, and the ways modern attention gets fragmented. The talk gradually turns toward steadiness, embodied concentration, and a softer relationship to experience, ending with a quiet emphasis on surrender, simplicity, and peace.
Chapters: 00:00 Resting In Silence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=0 04:11 Clinging And Safety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=251 06:32 When Consciousness Lands - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=392 09:45 Samadhi As Fruit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=585 13:44 Restlessness And Worry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=824 21:57 The Self At Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=1317 27:57 Thought Without Shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=1677 35:07 Equanimity And Pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=2107 40:56 Practice In Daily Life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=2456

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver explores how equanimity and samadhi grow from letting go rather than from effortful control. He moves from the Buddha’s language of clinging and non-clinging into a practical discussion of worry, self-referential thought, and the ways modern attention gets fragmented. The talk gradually turns toward steadiness, embodied concentration, and a softer relationship to experience, ending with a quiet emphasis on surrender, simplicity, and peace.
Chapters: 00:00 Resting In Silence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=0 04:11 Clinging And Safety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=251 06:32 When Consciousness Lands - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=392 09:45 Samadhi As Fruit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=585 13:44 Restlessness And Worry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=824 21:57 The Self At Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=1317 27:57 Thought Without Shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=1677 35:07 Equanimity And Pleasure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=2107 40:56 Practice In Daily Life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-56706/?t=2456
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: The Role of Sila, Samadhi and Panna in Interpersonal Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how sila, samadhi, and panna show up in everyday relationship, not just on retreat. He begins with learning as a lifelong, whole-life path, then turns to vulnerability, speech, feedback, and the ways ego hardens around contact with others. The talk closes by showing how loosening self-view can make us more trustworthy, more open, and more able to love without fixing or clinging.

item.title: Removing the Thorns of Memory and Becoming
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores memory as something practice does not erase but gently transforms. He moves from present-moment awareness into the way memory, habit, and autobiography shape suffering, then widens to mortality and the craving to become something else. The talk settles into a quiet argument that safety, relinquishment, and openness can let the past be digested and the future feel less fearsome.

item.title: Seven Factors of Awakening
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver lays out the seven factors of awakening as a living sequence rather than a checklist, beginning with mindfulness and investigation and moving through energy, delight, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity. He keeps returning to the feel of practice in the body and mind: how curiosity opens inquiry, how effort becomes relaxed perseverance, and how equanimity makes room for the messiness of human life. Several brief references — including Sīdō Tejaniya, Gil Fronsdal, Sharon Salzberg, Leonard Cohen, Heather Havrilesky, Michelle McDonald, and Shinzen Young — help ground the teaching in concrete examples and personal stories.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=0 06:01 Seven factors overview - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=361 08:29 Learning mindfulness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=509 11:32 Equanimity and balance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=692 18:07 Softening into experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=1087 24:06 Tolerating ambivalence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=1446 28:00 Curiosity and investigation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=1680 34:43 Energy, delight, calm - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=2083 43:03 Collected mind, not thought - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-10075/?t=2583

item.title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto teaching about why stopping feels difficult: the self stays busy trying to find ground, while practice asks for a deeper loving acceptance of experience as it is. He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.

item.title: Impressionistic vs Precise Technique
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the tension between impressionistic and precise meditation instructions, arguing that practice needs both openness and discernment. He moves from a broad reflection on how teaching works into a clear three-part method for noticing feeling, images, and inner talk, then shows how that triad can help untangle selfing and support healing. The talk returns repeatedly to memory, pain, and the possibility of blessing the past through patient, attentive practice.

item.title: Monday Night Dharma Talk
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames mindfulness as a living curiosity rather than a duty, arguing that practice begins where the student actually is. He then develops that theme through investigation, honesty, and the willingness to look closely at craving, delusion, secrecy, shame, and the ways we manage our self-image. The talk closes by turning outward: other people, especially trusted feedback, help reveal blind spots and make the heart more transparent and trustworthy.
Chapters: 00:00 Curiosity Over Discipline - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=0 05:49 The Engine of Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=349 10:47 Do Not Force Interest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=647 15:13 Investigation as Learning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=913 25:25 Seeing Suffering Clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=1525 32:58 Inquiry and Distance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=1978 38:41 Secrets and Honesty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=2321 47:12 Feedback and Disorientation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55433/?t=2832
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Practice Note: Note your life away
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a brief practice note on noting: silently labeling experience to steady attention, but also to shift how experience is held in awareness. He develops the idea that what is noted is no longer quite “our life,” so practice becomes a way of stepping out of identification and seeing experience as arising and passing. The talk closes by widening the lens to body, room, feeling, and thought, ending with the phrase that we may be “noting our life away.”

item.title: Dharmette: Maturing our autobiography
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how mindfulness helps us step out of self-reinforcing stories without dismissing the value of narrative altogether. He argues that practice can mature our autobiography: making our life story more coherent, less rigid, and gentler toward what was once too painful or confusing to hold. The talk moves from neuroimaging and the limits of rumination into a quieter account of remembering, reinterpreting, and blessing the past with Dharma.
Chapters: 00:09 Stepping out of narrative - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=9 01:43 Why stories limit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=103 03:02 Space around thought - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=182 04:39 Maturing the story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=279 06:07 A jagged autobiography - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=367 08:16 Memory in practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=496 09:56 Remembering with Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=596 11:42 Putting the story down - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9859/?t=702
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Compulsive Thinking, Concentration & Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores why the mind so often feels restless, fragmented, and compelled to think its way through everything, especially in a culture that rewards constant stimulation. He argues that concentration and equanimity develop together: steadiness softens the urge to control experience, and equanimity makes room for feeling without friction. The talk moves from the ordinary habits of worry and self-narration into a quieter, more practical account of letting go, where peace is not numbness but a less defensive way of meeting life.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver explores why the mind so often feels restless, fragmented, and compelled to think its way through everything, especially in a culture that rewards constant stimulation. He argues that concentration and equanimity develop together: steadiness softens the urge to control experience, and equanimity makes room for feeling without friction. The talk moves from the ordinary habits of worry and self-narration into a quieter, more practical account of letting go, where peace is not numbness but a less defensive way of meeting life.
Chapters: 06:15 What experience becomes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=375 10:54 Samadhi as letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=654 18:51 Worry and modern life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=1131 28:27 A sheltered mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=1707 33:52 Thought’s false gravity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=2032 40:03 Silence without defense - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=2403 46:10 Pleasure, attachment, and restraint - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=2770 52:41 Enough, and waking up - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=3161 59:10 Closing reflections - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-55103/?t=3550

item.title: Reflections on Sila
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on sila as more than a list of rules: a lived path of non-harming, self-knowledge, humility, and the willingness to be changed by suffering. He moves from the image of becoming a refuge for all beings into questions of grief, forgiveness, emptiness, and how ethical life deepens as the mind becomes more unified. The talk then turns toward modern responsibility, including the pull between self-protection and widening concern for others, animals, and future generations.

item.title: Technology as an Existential Threat to Mindfulness - Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando examine how smartphones, apps, and the attention economy shape mindfulness, restlessness, and even our sense of self. The first half builds the case that modern technology trains habit, urgency, and fragmentation; the second half turns practical, with suggestions for simpler defaults, gentler boundaries, and more humane ways of using devices. A short guided experiment and discussion sections help make the point concrete without turning the talk into a polemic.

item.title: Monday Night Dharma Talk
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores grief as a central mechanism of Dharma practice: not a detour from awakening, but part of how the heart matures through letting go. He moves from a retreat on anger that revealed grief, through poetry and teacher quotations, into a clear argument that non-clinging often feels like loss before it feels like freedom. The talk widens from personal practice to collective life, then closes with comments and a short period of sitting that emphasizes radical permission for whatever the heart is carrying.

item.title: Sila as Our Practice in the World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver speaks about sila as the way practice shows up in ordinary life: in attention to suffering, in how we relate to others, and in the habits that shape our conduct. He starts from vulnerability and dependency, then moves through mindfulness, non-clinging, feedback, and the courage to revise our views when they harden into self-protection. The talk closes by widening ethics beyond private self-improvement into concern for others, future generations, and a life that can be of benefit without needing certainty about outcomes.
Chapters: 00:00 Vulnerability and dependence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=0 02:23 Belonging and mindfulness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=143 06:36 Ethics as the practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=396 10:30 Love, clinging, and letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=630 16:00 Wisdom and humility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=960 24:25 Feedback and self-view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=1465 29:07 Freefall and courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=1747 36:19 Moral incoherence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=2179 41:28 Radical hearts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-53265/?t=2488

item.title: Untitled (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses Martha Nussbaum’s account of human dependence to open a wider reflection on shame, vulnerability, and ethics. From there he moves through mindfulness, sila, and the way practice shows up in ordinary conduct, arguing that ethical life is where the Dharma becomes most concrete. The talk settles into questions of confidence, feedback, defensiveness, and moral incoherence, and ends by returning to the possibility of a life of benefit without grand certainty.

item.title: Sīla, Samādhi, Pañña
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames the Buddhist path through sīla, samādhi, and paññā as three inseparable expressions of one training, grounded in love rather than moral performance. He moves from the ordinary hindrances and the Four Noble Truths into a patient account of ethics, collectedness, and wisdom, using images of refuge, the face, and ownership to show how practice softens fear and self-clinging. The talk steadily widens from personal suffering to the way a heart becomes safer, clearer, and more available to others.

item.title: Practice Notes
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a short set of practice notes focused on how to work with the mind in meditation. He emphasizes that practice is often personal and idiosyncratic, and that what helps in one moment may be a reminder, a reassurance, an inquiry, or a gentle check on clinging. The talk closes by pointing back to what is already good and present, rather than turning practice into an endless project of self-improvement.
Chapters: 00:30 Opening Practice Notes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9287/?t=30 01:15 Personal Practice Discussions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9287/?t=75 02:44 Why Guidance Must Fit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9287/?t=164 03:51 Learning Inner Pep Talks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9287/?t=231 04:49 Energy, Gentleness, Inquiry - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9287/?t=289 05:56 Returning To Present Goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9287/?t=356
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: The Human Condition
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how hard it can be to hold the full range of being human: grief, joy, climate anxiety, small pleasures, and the sense that they don’t neatly fit together. He then describes a meditation-related experience in which tears, laughter, and a surprising underlying peace arose at once, suggesting a more spacious way of meeting suffering. The talk ends with a quiet recognition that the human condition is larger, stranger, and more workable than it first appears.
Chapters: 00:11 Being Human - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=11 01:18 One Moment at a Time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=78 02:27 What Happens in the News - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=147 02:43 A Practice Setting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=163 03:35 Tears and Compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=215 04:57 Laughter in Grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=297 06:45 Peace Underneath - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=405 08:03 The Human Condition - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=483
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9288

He then describes a meditation-related experience in which tears, laughter, and a surprising underlying peace arose at once, suggesting a more spacious way of meeting suffering. The talk ends with a quiet recognition that the human condition is larger, stranger, and more workable than it first appears.
Chapters: 00:11 Being Human - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=11 01:18 One Moment at a Time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=78 02:27 What Happens in the News - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=147 02:43 A Practice Setting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=163 03:35 Tears and Compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=215 04:57 Laughter in Grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=297 06:45 Peace Underneath - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=405 08:03 The Human Condition - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9288/?t=483

item.title: Ethical Implications of Mindfulness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver speaks about mindfulness as cultivation rather than quick self-assessment, returning again and again to the idea that practice changes us gradually and often invisibly. From there he broadens the talk into ethics: how insight shows up in speech, relationships, generosity, and our sense of responsibility to other beings. The talk moves between personal practice, Buddhist references, and secular moral arguments, ending with a quiet reminder to practice without needing applause.
Chapters: 00:00 Measuring Progress - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=0 03:21 Little by Little - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=201 05:44 Conscience and Responsibility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=344 08:49 Compassion Needs Action - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=529 10:33 Insight Must Be Lived - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=633 17:15 Practice in Relationship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=1035 28:56 Compassion and Giving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=1736 36:23 Doing the Most Good - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=2183 42:36 Without Expecting Applause - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50747/?t=2556

item.title: Brian Lesage, Matthew Brensilver: Dharma Debate: Samvega versus Softening and Receiving (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
This talk explores a tension at the heart of Dharma practice: the pull of urgency, renunciation, and effort on one side, and softening, receiving, and ease on the other. Matthew Brensilver frames the question through the Buddha’s image of crossing the flood without pushing forward or staying in place, then turns to samvega, the backward step, and the ordinary textures of life as places where practice actually unfolds. The talk moves between teaching, personal story, and a closing poem to suggest that freedom is not somewhere else, but found in meeting experience without grasping it or hardening against it.
Chapters: 00:00 The Middle Path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=0 02:00 Urgency and Ease - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=120 07:45 Crossing the Flood - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=465 10:05 Samvega and Dread - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=605 18:44 Burnout and Softening - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=1124 26:54 The Backward Step - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=1614 33:14 Curiosity and Renunciation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=1994 40:09 Freedom in Ordinary Life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=2409 42:16 What the Living Do - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-50687/?t=2536

item.title: Samadhi (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi as a natural settling of the mind rather than something forced by will. He moves from the burdens of fragmented attention and habitual storytelling into the ways concentration supports mindfulness, insight, and a quieter relation to experience. The talk closes with practical encouragement: patience, non-striving, and allowing the mind to gather on its own terms.

item.title: Metta Reflections: Opening to Life Requires Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames metta as more than a feeling: it can cultivate love, steady concentration, or bring buried aversion to the surface for purification. He then widens the teaching by showing how insight practice and loving-kindness interweave, using a teen retreat story to illustrate how simple care can reach resistance more directly than force. The talk closes in a quiet metta instruction, emphasizing humility, the vulnerability of being human, and the ordinary worthiness of care.

item.title: Delusion
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores delusion as one of the three roots of suffering, distinguishing it from greed and aversion and showing why it is often harder to notice. He moves from personality tendencies and everyday self-deception into mindfulness, humility, and the willingness to be wrong, then closes by emphasizing feedback, ethical conduct, and the need to become safer for ourselves and others. The talk stays practical throughout, with examples from relationships, ambivalence, and the subtle ways the mind rationalizes its own patterns.
Chapters: 00:21 Three Roots - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=21 02:06 Delusion in Daily Life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=126 06:38 What Delusion Feels Like - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=398 11:41 Humility and Self-Deception - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=701 16:56 Willingness to Know - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=1016 21:50 Wise View and Rationalizing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=1310 27:11 Ambivalence and Confusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=1631 33:05 Signs and Antidotes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=1985 40:56 Lowering the Waterline - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8976/?t=2456

item.title: Practice Note: Noticing Something New
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a short practice note on attention: our meditation can become familiar and efficient, but it can also be a way of looking anew. He uses the image of a theater of the mind to point to the habits that shape experience, and then invites listeners to ask what might be seen if those habits were loosened. The emphasis is not on forcing insight, but on settling enough to meet experience with fresh eyes.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Context - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8898/?t=0 00:30 Grooved Attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8898/?t=30 01:00 Practice And Familiarity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8898/?t=60 01:49 Looking In New Ways - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8898/?t=109 02:49 Letting The Frame Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8898/?t=169 03:28 Seeing With New Eyes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8898/?t=208
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Worrying and Tranquility
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores worry as a familiar strategy for managing the insecurity of being human, then turns toward tranquility as something supported by conditions rather than forced by will. He moves from the basic texture of restlessness to three practical supports: simplifying commitments, spending time in nature, and reducing compulsive technology use. The talk stays close to ordinary life, with a quiet emphasis on how attention, clinging, and environment shape the mind.

item.title: Samadhi
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi as a quiet gathering of the mind that comes less from forcing concentration than from relaxing friction with experience. He links attention to restlessness, technology, narrative thinking, and the habit of waiting for a better future, then turns toward the ways practice can feel safe without words. The talk closes with teachings on letting go, including brief references to Shinzen Young, Daniel Kahneman, Joseph Goldstein, Suzuki Roshi, and Ajahn Brahm.

item.title: Grief
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores grief as a central part of letting go, rather than something separate from practice. He moves from personal reflections and poetic quotations into a wider view: grief as a response to love, attachment, impermanence, and the limits of control. The talk also widens into collective grief, mortality, and the way Dharma practice can make room for vulnerability without rushing past it.

item.title: Concluding talk and Q&A
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver closes the retreat by reflecting on practice as a long process of maturation rather than a sudden finish. He ties the teaching to impermanence, suffering, and not-self, then moves into practical questions about retreat rhythm, staying connected with others, and how silence should end gradually rather than abruptly. The talk ends with a quiet emphasis on generosity, gratitude, and carrying the retreat’s values back into ordinary life.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening gratitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=0 01:56 Three characteristics - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=116 04:16 Maturation through practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=256 08:03 Ordinariness and humility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=483 10:11 Stability and confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=611 12:09 Serious, not grim - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=729 14:55 Retreat rhythm - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=895 21:40 Quiet and connection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=1300 30:16 Generosity as the field - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8751/?t=1816

item.title: Talk: Insight into Dukkha
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses the film After Life, Leonard Cohen, and a series of Dharma and contemporary references to ask whether any moment of experience can be enough. From there he turns to dukkha as a plain feature of being human, not a personal failure, and shows how careful attention to suffering can become a basis for insight rather than despair. The talk moves gradually toward equanimity, softness, and a happiness less dependent on conditions.

item.title: Talk: Cultivating Well-being for Insight
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why Dharma practice begins close to ordinary human vulnerability, not with abstract theories. He moves from the need for safety and ethical restraint into concentration, loving-kindness, and peace as forms of well-being that make insight more bearable. The talk circles back to the idea that a stable, blameless, loving life can hold more truth without becoming overwhelmed.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening with the rain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=0 01:05 Practice starts close in - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=65 04:51 Insight and contingency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=291 08:36 Titrating truth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=516 13:05 Safety, ethics, refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=785 18:07 A field of non-harming - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=1087 23:07 Blamelessness and transparency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=1387 26:29 Gathering the mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=1589 30:47 Rapture and loving-kindness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8742/?t=1847

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Closing Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
In this closing session from a Spirit Rock retreat, Matthew Brensilver and Sylvia Boorstein move from the mood of the day into a quiet, practical reflection on love, grief, and ethical response. The talk weaves together Martin Luther King Jr., Leonard Cohen, Norman Fischer, Nyanaponika, Thomas Merton, Shantideva, and a Zen story about the monk and the tiger, using them to point toward compassion, humility, and a life that practice steadily becomes. The final stretch opens into a communal dedication of merit, with a gentle reminder that retreat ends by carrying kindness back into ordinary life.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into the Day - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=0 05:05 Love With Strength - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=305 13:38 Treaty and Ethical Grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=818 20:07 Refuge Becomes Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=1207 30:28 Zen Paradox and Freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=1828 36:58 Purpose in Daily Life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=2218 53:30 Aspiration Without Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=3210 01:01:32 The Tiger and the Vine - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=3692 01:17:56 Dedication and Departure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-48357/?t=4676
