item.title: Bhavatanha Mortality and Letting Go
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores bhava tanha, the craving for existence, through mortality, selfhood, and the ways the mind keeps arranging a future. He moves from a film about the dead choosing one life moment, to Buddhist and psychological perspectives on death anxiety, identity, and clinging. The talk stays practical: what changes when we sit as if there were no future, and when the self is seen less as a solid fact than as something conditioned by fear and grasping.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Framing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=0 01:48 The Film Premise - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=108 04:15 Mortality and Tension - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=255 09:30 Existence Bias - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=570 15:50 Death Anxiety Shapes Culture - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=950 20:29 Sitting Like the Dying - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=1229 26:15 Bhava Tanha in Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=1575 33:46 Self as Conditioned - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=2026 36:24 Cessation of Becoming - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8504/?t=2184

item.title: Monday Night Meditation Talk (Drop-in at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores delusion as the hardest of the three poisons to notice, moving from recent research on greed, aversion, and confusion into close, practical reflections on how self-story, certainty, and secrecy keep the mind from seeing clearly. He folds in voices from Catherine Schultz, U Tejaniya, Philip Moffitt, and others to show how honesty, humility, and feedback can become part of meditation rather than obstacles to it. The talk closes with a quiet emphasis on learning from life without self-punishment, and on how that learning can soften the heart.
Chapters: 00:00 Research and the three poisons - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=0 03:14 Delusion as habit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=194 08:09 Water we cannot see - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=489 11:52 Willingness to be wrong - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=712 17:48 Wise view and self-story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=1068 26:41 Meditating with honesty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=1601 34:23 Disruption as opportunity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=2063 47:48 Humility in the heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=2868 55:47 Keep looking - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-47160/?t=3347
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Practice Note
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a brief practice note on what mindfulness can actually do: even when attention meets difficulty, something in the heart tends to relax. He speaks candidly about mindfulness as a real turning toward the moment, not a half-hearted technique, and suggests that awareness can bring a sense of inner safety and surrender. The talk ends by inviting listeners to notice the part of being that is soothed by awareness itself.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening note - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8228/?t=0 00:19 Attention relaxes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8228/?t=19 00:54 Difficulty and safety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8228/?t=54 01:24 Turning toward the moment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8228/?t=84 02:08 Not half-hearted - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8228/?t=128 02:34 Surrender and soothing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8228/?t=154
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores why Dharma language can seem paradoxical, especially around effort, letting go, selfhood, and attainment. He then uses a New York Times essay on “living in the present” and Aristotle’s distinction between telic and atelic activities to clarify why some forms of practice are complete only in the doing. The talk closes by bringing that distinction back to meditation, contrasting Zen non-striving with more goal-oriented insight language while suggesting that practice is, in its lived moment, deeply atelic.
Chapters: 00:15 Dharma and paradox - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=15 02:08 One world, not two - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=128 03:48 A present-moment essay - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=228 04:45 Telic and atelic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=285 06:54 What present means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=414 08:27 Practice and attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=507 10:07 Meditation question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=607 11:16 Here, not elsewhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=676
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8229

He then uses a New York Times essay on “living in the present” and Aristotle’s distinction between telic and atelic activities to clarify why some forms of practice are complete only in the doing. The talk closes by bringing that distinction back to meditation, contrasting Zen non-striving with more goal-oriented insight language while suggesting that practice is, in its lived moment, deeply atelic.
Chapters: 00:15 Dharma and paradox - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=15 02:08 One world, not two - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=128 03:48 A present-moment essay - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=228 04:45 Telic and atelic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=285 06:54 What present means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=414 08:27 Practice and attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=507 10:07 Meditation question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=607 11:16 Here, not elsewhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8229/?t=676

item.title: Perils and Promises of the First Day
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames the first full day of retreat as a time when moods, fatigue, restlessness, and doubt can easily color everything, and he urges listeners not to confuse that early turbulence with the value of practice. From there he widens the view: retreat is not about chasing pleasantness, but about learning to meet suffering, craving, and imperfection with equanimity, curiosity, and a steadier heart. He closes by emphasizing that confidence in the path is less about getting life right than about starting, continuing, and caring for the present moment well.
Chapters: 00:00 First Day Reality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=0 01:08 Mood, Not Truth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=68 04:42 Peace and War - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=282 07:15 Pleasures of the Path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=435 14:03 Hardship with Purpose - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=843 18:28 Wanting, Aversion, Fatigue - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=1108 28:04 Change and Responsibility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=1684 32:30 Equanimity and Karmic Knots - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=1950 43:09 Completeness and Confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-8128/?t=2589

item.title: Surrender, Power, Sila
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores surrender as a central but uneasy part of Dharma practice: how retreat conditions soften defenses, how mindfulness itself involves letting go of ownership, and why surrender needs to be balanced with discernment. He moves through Augustine, Nietzsche, and a satirical Onion headline to show the wide range of meanings power and surrender can take, then turns to the risks of teacher misuse and the need for sila. The talk closes with a quieter emphasis on safe surrender, personal agency, and using power for the welfare of others.
Chapters: 00:22 Retreat and Surrender - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=22 02:33 Surrender and Power - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=153 05:35 Augustine and Nietzsche - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=335 08:24 Giving Power Away - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=504 11:30 Trust and Caution - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=690 15:40 Mindfulness as Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=940 20:25 Teachers and Boundaries - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=1225 25:39 Sila and Misuse - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=1539 33:05 Questions and Closing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7995/?t=1985

item.title: Bringing Dharma Into Interpersonal and Communal Life (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins by questioning the habit of measuring retreat by surface-level mental states, and argues that practice is often changing more deeply than we can easily see. From there he widens the frame: retreat and ordinary life are inseparable, insight has to be lived, and silence has ethical implications in speech, relationship, and community. He closes by linking meditation to a broader bodhisattva-like responsibility, using examples from social ripple effects, effective altruism, and Norman Fischer on acting without expecting applause.
Chapters: 00:00 Measuring retreat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=0 06:27 Fog and gradual change - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=387 09:00 Practice becomes life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=540 11:19 What silence reveals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=679 12:42 Compassion must act - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=762 18:12 Speech and relationship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=1092 26:38 Softening self-view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=1598 32:31 Widening care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=1951 37:05 Justifying a life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41183/?t=2225
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Intentions for Practice and the Jewel of Curiosity (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a reflective talk on what actually sustains practice over time: first urgency, then trust, and finally a quieter curiosity that keeps the heart engaged. He moves from a small mouse-in-a-trap story into questions of renunciation, mindfulness, equanimity, and the subtle ways practice can still be clouded by aversion. Toward the end, he turns to story itself—asking whether we can live less as a character in a script and more as awareness knowing experience directly.
Chapters: 00:00 Mouse as mirror - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=0 02:55 Practice and trust - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=175 09:36 Urgency and renunciation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=576 15:38 Love and freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=938 20:16 Curiosity becomes mindfulness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=1216 25:06 Fresh attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=1506 30:59 Equanimity and knots - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=1859 38:36 Beyond story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-41128/?t=2316

item.title: Day 1: Metta for Self (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver introduces a day of self-directed metta practice by gently distinguishing loving-kindness from mindfulness, then suggesting that the clearer we see human vulnerability, the more natural care becomes. He returns to the Buddha’s instruction to “make of yourself a refuge,” using it to frame self-love as steadiness, safety, and reliability rather than self-indulgence. The talk then moves into the felt practice of repeating simple phrases, listening for their effect in the body, and recognizing that even resistance or harshness can become part of the practice.

item.title: Clinging, letting go and the path of sila, samadhi and panna
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores letting go as the thread running through meditation, ethics, relationship, and wisdom. Starting from a Joseph Goldstein anecdote, he asks what it means to live without clinging, then moves through ownership, self-concern, and the way love can be separated from possession. The talk closes with classical sutta imagery and a quiet case that when nothing is taken as "mine," experience can feel more open, spacious, and responsive.
Chapters: 00:00 A Dharma in one sentence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=0 04:12 Mindfulness as non-clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=252 06:21 The habit of ownership - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=381 10:48 Dana and the feel of giving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=648 15:22 Love without control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=922 20:25 Security and intimacy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=1225 27:15 Open air and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=1635 30:56 The threefold path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=1856 36:46 Nothing lands - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7547/?t=2206

item.title: Attention, Love, and Service
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how attention, love, and service are woven together in practice: we learn to notice the goodness already here, face the mind’s negativity bias, and open to the intensity of being human without flinching. He moves from mindfulness and equanimity into the question of how love becomes service when it loosens its grip on clinging, control, and self-importance. The talk closes with reflections on mortality, freedom, and the possibility of being radically available to what is needed.
Chapters: 00:10 Goodness and Negativity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=10 03:34 It's Already Here - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=214 07:43 Shared Suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=463 13:15 Attention as Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=795 18:28 Seeing Human Life Clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=1108 26:22 From Insight to Service - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=1582 31:25 Love Without Clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=1885 36:01 Mortality and Freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=2161 38:46 Closing Reflection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7488/?t=2326

item.title: Intensity, Clinging and Anatta in the teen years (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver speaks to teenagers about intensity, self-consciousness, and the habits of clinging that make life feel tighter than it needs to be. He moves from grief and listening, to the pressure of performing a self, and then into a gentle argument for letting go as a trainable part of practice. The talk ends by widening that teaching into love, compassion, and a less defended relationship to who we are.
Chapters: 00:00 Grief Softens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=0 02:01 Trusting Young People - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=121 05:28 Being Heard - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=328 08:38 Intensity of Being Human - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=518 10:51 Performing a Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=651 15:54 Clinging and Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=954 20:00 Love Without Possession - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=1200 25:04 Freedom and Compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=1504 31:52 The Me Flag Relaxes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38545/?t=1912

item.title: Rhythms and Refinements of Practice
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the long, uneven unfolding of Dharma practice: the early sense that something meaningful is being touched, the surprise of being changed in ways that are not always visible, and the gradual refinement of motivation over time. He moves from personal story into a broader frame about unknowns, nonlinearity, and the way practice can deepen into love, service, and non-regret. The talk also draws on Leonard Cohen, Donald Rumsfeld, Rilke, Shinzen Young, and brief stories about other teachers to illuminate different facets of the path.
Chapters: 00:43 A wish for peace - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=43 02:13 First contact with practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=133 05:38 Early retreat and depth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=338 08:01 Unknowns on the path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=481 11:29 Subtle transformation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=689 14:37 Recalibrating intention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=877 18:31 Practice for others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=1111 24:59 Progress without arrival - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=1499 28:16 A loose Dharma map - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7345/?t=1696

item.title: Spirit Rock-Attention, Love and Service
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver traces a quiet arc from the pull of self-improvement toward a more spacious practice grounded in attention, love, and service. Drawing on Joseph Goldstein, Ajahn Sumedho, Marsha Linehan, Rilke, Marina Abramović, and Leonard Cohen, he keeps returning to the same point: looking closely can soften the heart, reveal shared vulnerability, and free energy for care. The talk closes by emphasizing that practice is not about perfection, but about starting, continuing, and letting love become less possessive.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice and what it serves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=0 02:20 Goodness already here - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=140 05:17 Beyond becoming - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=317 06:03 Service and mutuality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=363 12:00 Attention as love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=720 22:33 The gaze that reveals - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=1353 27:11 From empathy to freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=1631 34:38 Purifying care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=2078 42:02 Start and continue - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38168/?t=2522

item.title: Spirit Rock-Samadhi in the Context of Letting Go
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi not as a prize at the end of practice, but as something that grows out of letting go. He moves from the ordinary habit of waiting and reaching, through the hindrances, to the way settled attention makes life feel less fragmented and more complete. Along the way he brings in William James, Suzuki Roshi, Shinzen Young, Joseph Goldstein, Ajahn Brahm, Wittgenstein, and a closing Buddha teaching on releasing what is not ours.
Chapters: 00:00 Reversing the Question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=0 03:01 Samadhi and Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=181 07:23 Waiting for Nothing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=443 11:57 Beyond Ordinary Mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=717 16:55 The Uses of Samadhi - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=1015 24:01 Attachment and Confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=1441 30:13 Letting Go of Reference Points - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=1813 37:34 Stillness Without Will - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=2254 44:35 What Is Not Yours - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-38143/?t=2675

item.title: Spirit Rock-Metta in the Post Obama Era
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with an unexpected reflection on the political mood after the 2016 campaign, and then turns toward a simple but demanding aspiration: to be safe for others. From there he moves into loving-kindness practice as both a personal refuge and a way of meeting the pain, fear, and longing shared by others. The talk includes a guided metta sequence and closes by widening from self-care to a wish for peace and freedom for all beings.

item.title: Insight of Not Self and Relationships
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how insight into not-self often stays narrow until it is tested in relationship. He moves from psychotherapy's idea of generalization to the ways selfing shows up in eye contact, shame, family life, and love, using a few stories and references to show how ego quietly presses on others. The talk ends by widening not-self into not-other, suggesting that letting go can make room for more safety, tenderness, and ease in relationship.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice Beyond the Cushion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=0 02:16 Wisdom That Generalizes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=136 04:53 Relationship Reveals Habit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=293 07:58 Silence and Selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=478 13:24 Anatta in the Eyes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=804 19:01 Shame and the Empty Boat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=1141 23:53 Becoming Safe for Others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=1433 31:47 Not-Self and Not-Other - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=1907 34:54 Love, Possession, Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7192/?t=2094

item.title: Romance, Relationship, Sexuality & Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet, practical talk on how love, sexuality, and relationship become a genuine field of Dharma practice. He moves from the limits of retreat insight into the everyday tests of dependency, projection, aversion, and the need for belonging, while drawing on stories and quotations from Bob Thurman, Rilke, Stephen Mitchell, Lacan, and others. The talk gradually turns toward a workable vision of love: tender, clear about impermanence, respectful of the other’s freedom, and less burdened by self-view.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice Meets Relationship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=0 02:00 Relational Life as Mirror - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=120 07:28 Clinging and Belonging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=448 11:44 Love’s Difficulty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=704 15:37 Desire, Dependency, Risk - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=937 24:54 Eros and Freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=1494 36:37 Aggression and Not-Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=2197 48:27 Commitment and Refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7186/?t=2907
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Welcome and Opening Remarks
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens this retreat by framing practice as a sincere welcome to every part of experience, including joy, sorrow, and the parts of ourselves we usually resist. He moves from that tone of permission into the practical supports of retreat: community, silence, safety, beginner’s mind, and the way ethics and refuge shape the container for practice. The talk closes with the formal refuges and five precepts, then a quiet invitation to rest and begin the retreat gently.

item.title: JoAnna Hardy, Matthew Brensilver, Vinny Ferraro: Where You At, Where You Going
item.description:
This talk uses the Eightfold Path as a quiet map for noticing how people come to practice through different doorways: ethics, meditation, or wisdom. Matthew Brensilver and the other speakers move from personal stories of harm, shame, and service into a more balanced view of practice, emphasizing that the path is meant to stretch our strongest habits and invite the less-developed parts of ourselves. The conversation ends by turning that reflection into a practical exercise: name your strengths, notice your edge, and choose what to work with over time.
Chapters: 00:51 Eightfold Path Frame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=51 02:40 Three Doorways - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=160 05:36 Ethics and Turning Around - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=336 11:43 Precepts As Mirror - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=703 18:33 Friendship And Directness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=1113 22:28 Taking Refuge In Samadhi - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=1348 26:46 Dropping Into Experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=1606 33:28 Find Your Edge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=2008 37:58 Dyads, Silence, And Closing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-37207/?t=2278

item.title: Practice Note
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a brief practice note about how suffering can become so familiar that we stop noticing it, using a simple noise example to show how relief and ease can be easy to miss. He then turns to a Buddha teaching: pay attention to endings, fading away, and absence, not just arising and the next problem. The talk ends with a plain invitation to notice these shifts in walking and sitting, without making the practice complicated.
Chapters: 00:09 Familiar Noise - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=9 01:19 Ease Goes Unnoticed - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=79 01:58 Noticing Contentment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=118 02:44 The Fading Away - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=164 03:07 Knowing Absence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=187 03:38 Simple Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=218
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7001
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver offers a brief practice note about how suffering can become so familiar that we stop noticing it, using a simple noise example to show how relief and ease can be easy to miss. He then turns to a Buddha teaching: pay attention to endings, fading away, and absence, not just arising and the next problem. The talk ends with a plain invitation to notice these shifts in walking and sitting, without making the practice complicated.
Chapters: 00:09 Familiar Noise - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=9 01:19 Ease Goes Unnoticed - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=79 01:58 Noticing Contentment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=118 02:44 The Fading Away - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=164 03:07 Knowing Absence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=187 03:38 Simple Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7001/?t=218
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7001

item.title: Feedback & the Architecture of Self
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames the talk around Shinzen Young’s two maxims: don’t fight with yourself, and take feedback. From there he explores how criticism and misunderstanding can reveal the hidden architecture of self, showing how defensiveness protects self-view and how practice can gradually make us less brittle. He closes by linking this inner work to becoming safer, more open company for others.
Chapters: 00:31 Two Maxims - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=31 01:35 Learning From Feedback - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=95 02:58 Delusion and Self-Image - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=178 05:07 Seeing Self-Clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=307 07:10 Becoming Unoffendable - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=430 09:30 Tolerating Disorientation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=570 10:51 Safer For Others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-7002/?t=651
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7002
